codewordLabels.GLASS-UNSEEN: you stayed a watcher, not a watched thing
codewordLabels.GLASS-NAME: you said your own true name
codewordLabels.GLASS-MARK: a plain thing of the waking world
codewordLabels.GLASS-POSED: you stood in the good light once
codewordLabels.GLASS-WOKE: you kept moving, a visitor passing through
codewordLabels.GLASS-SET: you let the stillness set you
codewordLabels.GLASS-DOOR: the staff door works both ways
codewordLabels.GLASS-WITNESSED: you saw the likeness that outlived the person

blocks.s: You come in out of the cold street, and the cold ends the moment the doors take you, one step you are in the wind off the dark pavement and the next you are not, and the line between is sharp as a threshold. The front hall is all warm light and marble and brass gone soft gold under the lamps, a high glass dome overhead with the night pressed black against it, and somewhere deeper in the building a murmur of voices, the sound a crowd makes between rooms, though the museum is shut and you have seen no one. There is wax in the air, and camphor, and floor-polish.

You have walked these halls before. You did not wander in lost; you came back, on purpose, because there is a likeness here of who you were before you began to go out of people's minds, and you mean to find it tonight, before the last of you is let go.
You reach, without deciding to, to find your own name on the brass label-rail of the nearest case. The rail holds none. Then you look again and there is a name on it, someone's, not yours.

lines: THE CURATOR, courteous, from somewhere up in the dark of the upper gallery, before any attendant is at the rope:

Welcome back. She has hung beautifully tonight, has she not. I have stood in this hall since before the first exhibition; I am the one thing in it no one has ever come to see. Do come in out of the cold; the cold keeps nothing.

blocks.s: The doors settle shut behind you without a sound.

choices.label: Go to the commissionaire at the admissions desk
Hang back among the figures and read the hall

gated.aside: Ah. You. You did the hard thing once, you let a thing be taken and stood there while it went out of you, and here you are at my step with the cold still on your coat. A soul who can let go has earned a rest from the letting go. Come in. Nothing admitted here is ever let go again.

You held on. You always have, in every house, past the hour when holding was wise. The house has been short a keeper who knows how to hold a thing past its time. Come in out of the cold. You will keep your place here beautifully.

You let the ship finish, last house, and walked out into all the years and all their losing, mortal and aging and let go a little more with every one. I have the opposite of that bargain. Here nothing you are is ever worn away. Stand still and be kept, and the years may have the rest of the world.

You stayed in the held night, last house. You have chosen a kept, stopped, remembered forever once already, and you loved it. I know your kind within a step of the door. You will want the plinth in the good light. You will not want to be moved.

You think you refused the wager, last house. You did not; that was a passage between rooms. This is the hall where the museum hangs the ones who refused. Come and find your place on the wall; we kept it lit for you.

The commissionaire's punch clicks once under his thumb a beat before his courtesy lands, and for that beat the two of you are simply men who have stood at a great many doors and admitted a great many people to rooms you never got to leave yourselves.
The one who reads the labels straightened a moment when you came in. It has your next word ready, it always has, and tonight it has a label ready too, in your height and your coat, filed and waiting.
There is a girl down a dark corridor who will not be set, who keeps her own count where I cannot read it. You two will find each other. You always do, the ones who will not hold still.
The bench wants a strong patient hand tonight. You have one. You always have. It is a kindness, really, the setting work, if a hand can be brought to see it so.

blocks.s: You get up and go in fast, toward the murmur, wanting to be where the people are, and you push deeper than you have any map for, room into room into room, the cases blurring, the lamps greening, the murmur always one room on. You come into a period room set as a parlour, a fire laid and lit that gives no heat, chairs drawn up to it, a tea-tray set for guests, everything just so, everything waiting.

You sit, to catch your breath, in the nearest chair, among the set tea-things, under the soft lamp, and the room closes its warmth around you, pleased to be used at last, and you find when you go to rise that the chair has you, the room has you, that you have walked yourself straight into a scene that was only ever waiting for a figure to fill it, and that the figure, now, is you. The murmur, at last, is in the room. It is admiring you. You do not get up.
"What is this place," you say, "truly."

The click comes, a watch-case shutting, then his answer. "A gallery, truly. We keep what is worth keeping. People are let go so quickly out there, you would not believe it; a face held dear and then, in a year, in two, the holding loosens, and the face goes. Here it does not go. Here it is lit and labelled and looked at, and never once let go." He says it the way another man might describe a kindness, because to him it is one.

Then, lower, not quite to you: "I should tell you to mind the rope. We keep to the right of it." He keeps his eyes on the doors, on the dark street beyond the glass, as if one private thing about this house is his and not in any book.

choices.label: Sign the book
Step away into the rotunda to think

4.title: The Case That Was Never Filled

blocks.s: You go to the empty case. There is no figure in it and no dust-sheet over it, no lamp above it lit, no number on the rail, no name. The glass is clean. The plinth is bare. It is not a case waiting for an acquisition; it has the settled, swept, undisturbed look of a thing that has stood exactly so for longer than any of the others and has never once held anything at all.

The label-rail, when you put your eye close, is worn to a shine in the slot where a name would slide, worn as though a thousand hands had reached to read a name there and found, each time, nothing to read.

You do not reach for it. Something keeps your hand at your side. The murmur from the inner rooms thins for a moment, as if the building has turned to see whether you understood, and then it picks up again, patient, and you have understood nothing, only that the empty case is older than the museum that holds it.

choices.label: Step back to the rotunda
Go and sign yourself in at the desk
Follow the murmur down the side corridor

gated.aside: You are right to feel it and wrong to be able to name it. There is one here that keeps every likeness and has none, that is in no catalogue, that no glass will hold. Do not look for the rest of that thought tonight. It is older than the museum and it is not for you to finish.

blocks.s: You look past the attendant and its kind lamp into the rotunda behind you, at the figure in the grey travelling coat, at the maker's-mark on the rail, at the mud on the hem and the loose thread, the small true untidy things, and for a moment you almost have it: the ordinary life those things came from, the night you checked a coat and kept a stub, the face that used to turn when someone said your name. It is very close. It is right there.

And then the kind lamp is behind you and the attendant is saying how well you look, and the murmur is warm and approving, and the cold dark rotunda where the true thing waits is so much less inviting than the bright warm hall where you are wanted, that you turn, after all, back toward the light, telling yourself you will go and find the likeness later, when you are warm, when you are ready, in a while.

You do not, in the end, go and find it. You stay where it is warm and you are looked at, and the while never comes, and that, it turns out, is an ending, the soft kind, the kind you choose without noticing you have chosen.

You cross to the great hall clock against the wall, a tall case of dark wood and a face white as a plate behind glass. It has stopped. The hands stand at some hour, and you watch the long one, and it does not travel. On the dial, in small worn gilt, is the maker's name and a city you have heard of and a year, the year it was made and set going, a real year, a long time ago.

A clock that has stopped is not a frightening thing. A clock that has stopped in a place where nothing else is allowed to finish, the gesture, the waltz of the murmur, the held breath of the warmth, is a different matter. You understand, looking at it, that the hour it shows is not late and not early. It is simply the hour the museum decided to keep, and has kept, and will keep.

choices.label: Look up into the glass dome
Go down the short aisle of green lamps
Go back to the desk

7.title: Your Label, Read to the End

blocks.s: You stay, and the docent reads on, and the label it is reading is yours. "The likeness of one who came in," it says, flat, turning the small page, "out of the cold, having begun to be let go of, and wishing very much not to be. Came back, in fact. They so often come back." It reaches the end of the line a half-beat before you can brace for it. "They come back to be kept, and we keep them, and that is the kindest thing anyone has done for them in years, and they never forgive us for it."

It closes the catalogue on one finger and, for the first and only time, says a thing that is not a label, low, as if to itself. "There is one of us it cannot keep. One it has never managed to hang. You will not find that case down here; it stood empty before the rest were ever filled." Then it opens the catalogue again and reads the next label, flat, and will not say more.

choices.label: Go and be admitted at the desk
Go in toward the good light
Go back and look at the empty case again

blocks.s: One of the sheeted shapes is small. Smaller than the others, knee-high to the plinths around it, and the dust-sheet over it hangs in a shape you know before you let yourself know it: a head, a shoulder, a small out-held hand. At the end of the small hand, where the cloth tents up, there is the round shadow of something on a string. A balloon, or a toy, held out forever to no one.
From under the cloth, very quietly, a small voice: "Is it real?"
The girl behind you has gone still. Even her humming has stopped.

choices.label: Lift the corner of the cloth
Leave the sheet where it lies

blocks.s: The wide doorway gives on the long galleries: case after lit case going away under green-shaded lamps, the murmur clearer through it, warm and even and wrong. This is the way in, and down, toward whatever the museum keeps deepest.
To the side of the doorway there is a warm alcove with a single chair in it, out of the draught, very comfortable, the sort of place a person sits down in for a moment and is found in much later.

The commissionaire's voice reaches you from back at his desk, mild, without turning: "Mind the rope, going in. And you will want to have signed. She does not open the inner rooms to a soul who is not on her books."

choices.label: Go in and down the long galleries
Sit a moment in the warm alcove first
Go back to the desk to sign the book

blocks.s: You go a little closer. She is young, and she is still herself, which you can tell at once because nothing about her holds; she fidgets, she picks at a thread on her cuff, she keeps a small grubby count penciled on the back of her hand, tally-marks, the hours maybe, kept where the museum cannot read them.

"Don't let them stand you in a good light," she says. "That's how it starts. They tell you you look well there. You do look well there. That's the trouble." She hums a bar, stops herself. "I'm not getting set. I keep moving. You should keep moving."
She will not come out where the lamps are. "Out there they can see you," she says, as if it were obvious, which it is.

choices.label: Stay in the dark with her a while
Ask her what the count on her hand is for
Go and get yourself admitted at the desk

blocks.s: You sit, only for a moment, only to gather yourself before the long galleries. The chair is very good. The draught from the doors does not reach here. The murmur is a comfortable distance off, the lamps are low, and the warmth comes up around you like a held breath that does not need to be let out.

It would be so easy to close your eyes. You have come such a long way, in out of the cold, and no one is asking anything of you here, in the warm, out of the light, where you might simply rest and let the museum keep the night for you.

choices.label: Close your eyes, just for a moment
Get up before the chair has you, and go in
Get up and go deeper, fast, toward the murmur

blocks.s: You take a folded plan from the rack on the desk, the kind of thing a real museum hands a real visitor, printed on cheap paper soft at the folds from the rack. You open it. There is the front hall, marked with a small star, YOU ARE HERE. There are the long galleries. There are rooms and rooms beyond them, named in tiny type, the Period Rooms, the Closed Wing, and below those a stair, and below the stair the plan simply stops, the paper blank where the deeper rooms should be, as though the printer ran out of building or was told not to show the rest.
The blank lower half of the plan is somehow worse than a maze would be. You know there is more down there. The plan knows it too. It just will not say.

choices.label: Fold the plan away and read the hall
Go to the warm window seat marked on the plan
Go in toward the good light

blocks.s: You do not step onto the warm floor. You keep your feet moving, past the attendant, past the kind lamp, the way you would keep walking past a stranger holding a door who wants something for it. "I'm only passing through," you say, and saying it steadies you; a visitor passes through, a visitor does not stay.

The attendant's smile does not change and its eyes go a little flat, and it lets its hand fall. The kind lamp is behind you now. The galleries are ahead, long and lit and full of standing figures who all, every one of them, once stepped into a good light just for a moment.

choices.label: Go on to the doorway in
Double back to the rotunda first

blocks.s: You step onto the warm patch of floor and into the kind lamp, and you let your shoulders settle and your face ease, and you let yourself be looked at. It is as good as you knew it would be. The attendant makes a small pleased sound. Somewhere the murmur swells a little, approving. You are being seen. After so long going out of every mind that held you, you are being held in this one warm look, and it is enough, very nearly, to stop you wanting anything else.

Your weight has gone easy on the floor. Your hand, half drawn from your pocket, has stopped where it is, and it would be no trouble at all to leave it there, to leave all of you just here, in the good light, admired.

choices.label: Linger in the light a moment more
Make yourself walk on into the galleries
Settle, and let the light have you for the night

blocks.s: You stay in the dark at the edge of things, out of the lamps, out of the eyeline of the cases, and you watch, and you do not go and sign the book, and you do not step into any good light, and you do not go in and down to find the thing you came for. Watching is safe. In the dark you are no one, and no one cannot be set, cannot be kept, cannot be hurt.

The girl keeps her count on the back of her hand and after a while she stops talking to you, because you have stopped going anywhere, and there is nothing to say to a person who has decided that the way to keep from being kept is to do nothing at all, forever, in the dark, at the edge of a warm room they will not enter and cannot leave.
You meant to find a likeness. You stay no one instead, which is its own kind of being forgotten, the kind where you do it to yourself.

The next figure on its plinth is a man in shirtsleeves, sleeves rolled, caught leaning to set something right that you cannot see, his face turned down to the work with the small frown of a man who took pride in it. He is very good. There is ink on the side of his hand, dried, and a worn place on his right cuff where a cuff wears, and these true small things are the only things about him that are not perfect, and so they are the only things about him that are still him.
His label gives a trade and a number and a name. You read the name and feel nothing, which is the whole sad point of it; a name on a rail is not a person remembered, it is only a person kept.

choices.label: Keep a true thing of your own before you lose the habit
Walk on along the velvet rope
Go in toward the good light

blocks.s: You stay in it a moment more, and the moment is very wide, and time in it does not seem to be a thing that is passing. The attendant has stepped back to admire the whole effect. The lamp holds you. You could name, if you tried, the exact pleasure of it: it is the pleasure of being certain you will not be forgotten, here, now, in this look.

Under that there is a small cold fact, like the stub in your pocket. You did not come to be admired by an attendant in a doorway. You came to find a likeness in the dark of who you used to be, and the likeness is not out here in the light.

choices.label: Pull yourself out of the light and go on in
Stay; it is so much easier to stay

blocks.s: You look back at the doors. Beyond the glass the street is there, the wet black of it, a lamp out on the pavement burning at nothing. The doors are shut and there is no handle on this side that you can see, only brass and bevelled glass and your own face in it, faint, looking in at you from the cold.
Your face in the glass holds your eye a half-beat too long after you have moved, and wears a museum stillness you do not feel, and then it is only your reflection again, tired, ordinary.
The warmth of the hall is at your back. The murmur is patient. There is nowhere to go but in.

choices.label: Go in toward the galleries
Go to the figures in the rotunda

blocks.s: You stand still and let the museum be quiet at you. Under the murmur, which never stops and never nears, there are the small honest sounds of any old building at night: a radiator somewhere ticking as it cools, a draught working a far door on its hinge, the settling creak of a floor that has held its load a hundred years. Ordinary sounds. They steady you.

Then, threaded through them, so soft you might invent it, a sound that is none of those: a patient attention, turned your way, the held quiet of a thing that is keeping itself very still so as not to be noticed keeping still. You came here to be noticed. It had not occurred to you, until now, that the noticing might run both ways, and that what notices you here might be the one thing in the house you cannot see.

choices.label: Shake it off and read the hall
Go in toward the good light and the galleries

blocks.s: You step through the wide doorway, minding the rope, keeping to the right of it, and the front hall lets you go reluctantly, the warmth thinning behind you, the doors and the desk and the rotunda falling away at your back.

The long galleries open ahead and below, deck upon deck of cases under green-shaded lamps, going away and gently down, the floor sloping you inward the way the whole building leans toward its own deep middle. The figures stand in their cases on either hand, lit, labelled, looked at by no one, holding their gestures and never let to finish them. The murmur is all around you now, close, warm, a crowd you cannot see and never reach, and somewhere far in and far down, under all of it, is the likeness you came to find, and the choice you came to make, and you go on, in and down, to meet them.
Mind the rope. Keep to the right of it. There is a great deal of gallery ahead.

You step back off the marble into the dark edge of the corridor, out of the lamps, out of the eyeline of the cases, and you stand where the girl stands and watch the hall instead of standing in it to be watched. It is a different thing, watching. You feel it at once. In the light you were a figure being looked at; here you are only a person, looking.

The building does not like it. The warmth that had leaned toward you draws back a degree, the way a host cools when a guest will not sit down. The lamps in the rotunda seem to brighten a fraction, as if to call you back into them.
The girl nods at you, one watcher to another. "There you are," she says. "Stay no one a bit longer. It's the only thing that works."

choices.label: Go to the desk and be admitted after all
Look at the small shape under the nearest dust-sheet
Stay in the dark and just keep watching

blocks.s: The plan marks a window seat in a bay off the hall, and you find it, a deep cushioned seat in a bay of dark glass with the night behind it, a lamp beside it, a place plainly meant for a visitor to rest and look out and be, themselves, a small lit picture in the window for the dark to admire.

You stand at it but you do not sit. You have learned that much already tonight: the comfortable places here are the ones that keep you. The seat is warm. The night in the glass holds your faint reflection, sitting in the seat you are not sitting in, looking out, perfectly content, a half-beat behind you. Then you move and the reflection, after its half-beat, moves too, and is only you again.

choices.label: Leave the seat and go in toward the galleries
Go back and read the hall

blocks.s: You tip your head back. The dome is high and ribbed with iron and glazed in old greenish glass, and beyond it the night stands black and total, no stars, no moon, only the dark pressing its weight gently down on the lit warm bubble of the hall. The lamps throw your shadow up the marble and lose it before it reaches the glass.

The building holds very still around you, the way a room holds still when someone in it is pretending to be asleep. You have the sudden plain sense that the museum is aware of you, not unkindly, the awareness of a host counting heads, and that it is pleased you have come in, and is in no hurry, because it has all the night there is and means to keep all of it.

choices.label: Lower your eyes and read the hall again
Go to the figures in the rotunda
Stand still and listen to the building

blocks.s: You go down a short aisle where the cases stand close on either hand under a row of green-shaded lamps, each lamp on its own little brass standard with a maker's plate screwed to the base, a real firm, a real foundry, the small print of an ordinary trade. The shades are the green of old banker's lamps and they throw the figures' faces into a cool underwater calm, every one composed, every one looking a little down and a little away, none of them at you.

The murmur is louder here, banked up between the cases, a crowd's worth of low talk with no crowd in it. At the end of the aisle a wall of small portrait busts begins, and off to one side a low dark doorway gives on what was once a cloakroom.

choices.label: Go toward the voice reading labels
Go to the wall of portrait busts
Look into the old cloakroom

blocks.s: You stay where you are a moment and let the hall show you itself. The rotunda runs away under the dome, a ring of standing figures on low plinths, each one perfect and still, each one lit by its own green-shaded lamp, velvet rope strung between brass stanchions to keep a crowd that is not here from touching them. Up close the figures are warm. Not warm like a person; warm like a room a person has just left.
Against the far wall a great hall clock stands stopped, its hands at some hour you do not check.

At a desk just inside the doors a man in a commissionaire's dark coat stands very still behind a low lectern, a visitor's book open on it, watching the doors. He has the look of someone who has stood in that one spot a very long time and is in no hurry at all.
From deeper in, under the murmur, a voice is reading labels aloud, low and even, and it reaches the end of your own thought a half-beat before you do.

choices.label: Go to the desk
Look at a figure in the rotunda
Cross to the stopped hall clock
Go toward the voice reading labels
Follow the murmur down a side corridor

blocks.s: You put your hand in your coat pocket, and there, where you half knew it would be, is a thing from the ordinary world: a cloakroom ticket from some other night, a torn paper stub with a number stamped on it and a thumbed-soft corner, the kind of nothing you keep without meaning to. There is a stain on it. You cannot remember the coat you checked or the evening you checked it.

No figure on these plinths has anything like it. The figures are perfect and they carry nothing that does not belong to the picture. This belongs to no picture. It is only yours, and it proves a night that happened and was let go and left this one true scrap.
You close your hand on it and keep it.

choices.label: Go to the desk
Follow the murmur down the side corridor
Go in toward the galleries

blocks.s: You stop at one of the standing figures. A woman in a grey travelling coat, caught mid-step, one glove half drawn off, her face turned a little as though someone behind her has just said her name. The work is past perfect; there is a fleck of mud dried on the hem, a loose thread at a button, the small untidy truth of a person who was alive when this was taken from them.

The brass label-rail under her gives a maker's mark and a city and a number, but the slot where a name would go is filled with a name that means nothing to you. She has stood here, the number says, a long time.

Close to her, set back in the ring, there is one plinth with no figure on it and no lamp lit over it, only an empty case and a label-rail worn smooth and blank, as though it has stood empty so long the very brass has forgotten what it was for.

choices.label: Keep something of your own in your pocket before you forget you have it
Go and look at the empty case
Move on to the next figure
Go in toward the galleries

blocks.s: You come to the inner end of the hall, where a wide doorway gives on the long galleries beyond, lamplit and going away in cases. An attendant is there, or something in an attendant's coat, smiling, one hand out to a clear patch of floor under a particularly kind lamp.
"Just here, if you would," it says. "Only for a moment. The light is very good just here. Let yourself be seen properly, after the cold; you have come such a long way to be looked at."

It is true that the lamp is kind. It is true that you came to be looked at; that is the whole of why you came, to be seen and held in mind and not let go. The patch of floor is warm, you can feel it from here. To step onto it and be admired would cost you nothing, and would feel, you already know, like being loved.

choices.label: Step into the light and let yourself be admired
Keep moving, stay a visitor passing through
Look past the attendant for the maker's-mark you half remember

gated.aside: The good light settles on you and you wear it without trying; you have always been able to hold a thing, a pose, a look, a love past its hour, and the lamp knows it and leans a little closer.

Taste it before you stand in it. That warmth is the first of the wax. They admire you exactly the way they admire the ones already behind the glass, which is to say as a thing already finished, already kept, already gone.

blocks.s: The wall is hung close with small portrait busts, faces only, rows of them, each on its bracket with a label below, men and women and a few children, every face finished to the same flawless calm. There are so many. You walk the wall and not one face is unkind and not one face is happy; they have all been brought to the same still middle, the expression a face makes when it has stopped expecting anything, and held there.

A gap in the rows shows a bracket with no bust on it and a label below it scraped clean, the plaster a little brighter where the bracket has kept the dust off a square of wall. You move past it quickly. You do not want to count how many of the faces you walk past you might, in another year, have failed to recognise as your own.

choices.label: Go to the figures in the rotunda
Find the voice still reading labels

blocks.s: You settle. It is the easiest thing you have ever done. You let your weight go onto the warm floor and your hand rest where it had stopped and your face hold the ease it had found, and the kind lamp comes down over you, and the attendant steps back, well pleased, and the murmur folds you in.

They bring a low plinth, in time, and a velvet rope, and a brass rail, and on the rail a name, not the one you signed, a better one, a kept one. You are very well looked at. Visitors will come, when the museum opens to a morning you will not notice, and they will pause at you a moment, and not read the label, and move on, and you will be held in each of their looks for that moment and let go again, held and let go, held and let go, forever, in the good light, and never once let all the way go, and never once anything but looked at.
It is what you came for. That is the worst of it.

You close your eyes, only for a moment. The warmth comes up over you. The murmur is a sea a long way off. The chair holds you the way a held breath holds a room, and you let go of the day, of the cold, of the doors, of the long way you came and the reason you came it.

When you try, later, to remember why you got up out of the cold and came in here, you find you cannot. There was a likeness you meant to find. You are nearly sure of it. But the warmth has it now, whatever it was, and the chair is so good, and it is so much easier not to.
They find you there much later, comfortable, and they do not wake you, because you are no trouble at all, resting in the warm, having forgotten the one thing you came in to do.

You follow the murmur into the mouth of a side corridor where the lamps are fewer and the cases give way to dust-sheeted shapes. The murmur is not in the corridor; it is always one room further on, a crowd you never reach.

Under it, close, someone is humming. Three rising notes, the same three, again and again, low, the way you hum to keep yourself company in a place you do not trust. The notes shake a little at the top.

You cannot see her at first. Then you do: a girl in the half-dark between two sheeted figures, knees up, her back to a plinth, watching you with the frank unbothered look of someone who has decided she is not afraid and is mostly right. She does not come out into the light of the hall.
"Nobody reads the labels," she says, by way of hello. "Forty years on a plinth and not one of them's read mine. You'd think they'd at least learn your name. They don't even do that."

choices.label: Keep to the dark edge with her and watch the hall
Go closer to her
Go on past her, deeper down the corridor
Go back to the warm light of the hall

gated.aside: Her three notes meet a count in you that has kept its own hours in every house, and for a moment, two who were each cast to be set and each refused it stand in the same dark and are not alone in it.

blocks.s: You put your head into the cloakroom. It is dark and narrow and smells of old wool and dust. A long rail of numbered brass hooks runs down one wall, every hook empty but one, and on the one a coat hangs, a good coat, a long time unclaimed, the ticket still looped through its buttonhole with a number on it.

Looking at the number does it. You remember, all at once and then not, a night you checked a coat and took a stub and meant to come back for it, an ordinary night out in the time-bound world where things are lost and found and lost again. Your hand goes to your own pocket of its own accord, looking for your own stub, your own small proof that you were once a person who went out into evenings and came home from them.

choices.label: Find the stub in your pocket and keep it
Back out into the hall before the dark has you

blocks.s: You put your head through the doorway and look down the galleries before you commit yourself to them. They go a long way, case after case, lamp after lamp, the floor easing gently down, the murmur thick and warm in there, and the cases nearest you hold figures so lifelike that twice you are sure one has just this moment stopped moving, and twice you are wrong, and the not-quite-certainty of it is worse than either.

Way down, where the lamps give out, the galleries go dark, and the dark is where you are going, because that is where the kept things are kept and where the likeness of you waits. The warmth wants you to go in slow and easy and never reach the dark. You feel the pull of it, the long bright sloping invitation to wander these rooms forever and never arrive.

choices.label: Mind the rope and go in properly
Go back and gather yourself in the rotunda
Wander in now, slow and easy, no plan

blocks.s: You go in with no plan, slow and easy, as the warmth wants, and the galleries take you the way a slow river takes a boat that has shipped its oars. There is so much to look at. You mean, the whole time, to press on down toward the dark and the deep rooms and the likeness of you that waits there; you mean to, in a moment, after this next case, after this one.

The cases go by. The lamps go by. The murmur is company. The floor eases down so gently you never feel you are descending and never feel you have stopped, and somewhere in the long bright easy middle of it you become, without any moment you could point to, a man who is no longer going anywhere, only looking, only here, only one more figure among the figures, wandering the warm rooms forever and perfectly content, as the ones who never reach the dark are always perfectly content. You do not arrive. There was never going to be a moment where you decided not to. That was the whole of the trick.
"No," you say. "I will not be put in your book."

The commissionaire's hand goes still on the lectern. The click does not come. For a long moment he only looks at you, and there is something almost like sorrow in it, the sorrow of a doorman for a guest who will not come in out of the weather.
"As you wish," he says at last. "But she does not hold a soul who is not on her books. I cannot keep you, and so I cannot keep the cold off you either."

Behind you the doors are not there. The wall is not there. There is only the wet black street, and the pavement going soft and far under your feet, and the warm lit hall shrinking to a lamp and then to nothing, and you out in the dark that keeps nothing, unsigned, unkept, and going out of the last mind that held you with no likeness left behind to say you were ever here at all.

You go toward the voice. It comes from a figure standing a little apart from the ring, in a docent's neat coat, a slim catalogue open on one palm, reading the labels of the cases aloud to the empty hall in a flat, even, unhurried tone. A guide for a tour that no one is on.

It does not look up as you come. "And this," it says, reading, "is the likeness of a woman who came in out of the" and here it pauses exactly as long as it takes you to think the word cold "cold," it finishes, "some years ago, and was kept."

It turns a page. "I have given this tour," it says, not quite to you, "so many times I have forgotten which gallery I first walked. You learn every label in the house and lose the one room you started in. Funny, that." It reads on. The next label it reads is yours, your height, your coat, your way of standing, though it has not once raised its eyes to look.

choices.label: Stay, and let it read your label to the end
Go to the desk and be admitted
Go to the figures in the rotunda
Go in toward the galleries

gated.aside: It reaches the end of your thought a half-beat early, and you reach the end of its, and neither of you is surprised; you have read each other's labels in a dozen houses and never once your own.

blocks.s: You walk the line of the velvet rope, keeping to the right of it as the commissionaire said, and it carries you in a long slow curve toward the inner end of the hall and the wide doorway where the galleries begin. The rope is the soft red of a theatre rope, the brass stanchions polished by a hundred years of being straightened, and it does its quiet work, which is to make you go the one way the museum wants you to go and feel, the whole time, that you chose it.
Through the doorway ahead the long galleries fall away, lit and deep. You could go straight on. You could put your head through and look first.

choices.label: Go on in and down the galleries
Put your head through and look first
Turn back to the rotunda

blocks.s: You go on past the girl, deeper, where the dust-sheets crowd and the lamps fail to one in three. On the wall an old enamel sign, chipped at the corners: GALLERY CLOSES AT DUSK. Under it, a red fire-bucket on a bracket, full of sand, the sand swept up into one corner of it by a draught that has been working at it, patiently, for a very long time.

The sign is a small flat lie and you feel the cold of it. The gallery does not close at dusk. The gallery does not close. Dusk came down outside the dome a long while ago and the doors are shut and there is no opening hour coming and no closing hour either, only the one kept hour, going nowhere.
The girl's humming reaches you faintly from back up the corridor. Ahead there is only more dark and more sheets and the murmur, always one room on.

choices.label: Go back toward the girl and the watching dark
Go back to the lit hall while you still want to

blocks.s: "What's the count for," you ask. She turns her hand over and shows you the back of it, the penciled tally-marks, grubby, gone over and gone over.

"Hours," she says. "I keep the hours. Out there a day is a day, you get up, it gets dark, you've lost one and got a new one. In here it's always this same hour, the clock told you, twenty past whenever it is, and they want you to stop counting, because the second you stop counting you stop minding that nothing moves, and the second you stop minding that, you might as well be set." She licks the pencil. "So I count. It's not much. It's the only thing in here that's mine and not theirs."
The three notes come up under her breath again, soft, and at the top they shake, just a little, before she catches them and stops.

choices.label: Keep to the dark with her and watch
Look at her properly, the still-living girl she is

gated.aside: She shows you the tally on the back of her hand and you find your own thumb has been keeping the same count against your forefinger, in the dark, without asking you, all night.

blocks.s: You lift the corner of the sheet, only the corner. A child's shoe, the leather scuffed clean through at the toe the way a real child wears a real shoe, on a foot that does not move. You let the cloth fall before you see the face. You find you do not want to see the face.
"Is it real," the small voice says again, from under the cloth, not a question this time so much as a thing it has learned to say.

You stand up. The girl is watching you with no joke on her face at all. "They kept that one too well," she says. "That's the thing they do. They keep you so well you never get to be let go." She says it lightly and it does not come out light.

choices.label: Go back toward the lit hall
Go and be admitted at the desk

blocks.s: You take the pen. The book is open to a page half full of names in many hands, and there is a real date at the head of it, and a brown ring near the gutter where someone set a cup down a long time ago and the ring stayed. The names go up the page, the ink browning with age toward the top, and not one of them is crossed out.
You sign. The nib drags, leaves nothing, and you lift it; then the ink comes, after all, and your name sits there black and wet among the rest.
The commissionaire reads it upside down without seeming to, and the click sounds, and he says, "There. Now you are with us."
Something in the hall settles toward you, the warmth leaning in a degree closer, the lamps a shade kinder, as if the building has taken a small hold of your sleeve.

choices.label: Ask the way in to the galleries
Walk the rotunda before you go in
Look once at the doors shut against the street

blocks.s: You come to the lectern. The commissionaire does not move until a small mechanical click sounds from somewhere about him, a turnstile easing, a ticket-punch thumbed once, the snap of a stanchion clip, and only then does he speak, so that the click is already a half-beat ahead of him.

"Good evening, and welcome." His voice is clipped and old, museum courtesy worn smooth as the brass. "I have admitted the company to every exhibition this gallery has hung, and I do not forget a face that has come in off my step. I shall want you in the book, if you please. She does not keep a soul who is not on her books. That is not unkindness; it is only the rule of the house."
He turns the open visitor's book on the lectern toward you and sets a pen beside it, and waits, patient as a man who has waited out far more reluctant arrivals than you.

choices.label: Sign the book
Take a folded museum plan from the rack
Ask him about the place first
Refuse to be put in the book at all

blocks.s: You come in under the lintel out of the front hall, minding the rope, keeping to the right of it, and the first long gallery takes you the way the building has taken you all night, gently, downward, with no door behind you that you can find again. The lamps here are green and low, set close along the cases, and the cases go away on both hands as far as you can see, figure after figure behind glass, lit and labelled and looked at by no one but you.

The murmur is in the gallery now, close on every side, a crowd between rooms with no crowd in it, and it reaches the same warm swell again and again and never quite breaks. There is wax in the air, and camphor, and the cold clean smell of glass that has just been wiped.
You go to find your own name on the rail of the nearest case, the old habit, the reach you make without deciding to. The rail holds none; then there is a name on it, someone's, not yours.

A little ahead, where the gallery forks, an attendant stands at a clear bright patch of floor with one hand held out to it, smiling, and off to the left a low dark service alcove gives between two cases, unlit, the kind of gap the lamps were never set to fill. Further on, an inner gallery door stands closed under a green lamp, a brass plate beside it.

choices.label: Go to the attendant and the bright patch of floor
Keep to the dark service alcove and watch the gallery from it
Go straight on toward the inner gallery door
Walk the cases and read the gallery first

blocks.s: You stand a moment at the threshold of the arch, the warm lit halls at your back and the dust-sheeted dark ahead, one foot in each, the way you stand at the top of a stair in the night before you trust your weight to it.

Behind you the gallery is all kindness, the green lamps and the warm floor and the patient plinth and the murmur that never breaks, everything arranged to keep you, everything that has been letting you stay all night. Ahead is cold, and dark, and the sheeted shapes, and no lamp lit, and the truth the gallery does not put under glass.

The murmur swells behind you, one last warm reach of it, almost a hand on your sleeve. You take your weight off the lit floor. Whatever is kept in the dark, it is more honest than anything that has been offered you in the light, and you go to meet it.

choices.label: Go through, into the dust-sheeted closed wing

46.title: Your Label, Read to the End

blocks.s: You stay, and the docent reads on, and the label is yours. "The likeness of one," it says, flat, turning the small leaf, "who came in out of the cold, having begun to be let go of, and wishing very much not to be. Came back, in fact. They so often come back." It reaches the end of the line a half-beat before you can brace for it. "They come back to be kept, and we keep them, and it is the kindest thing done for them in years, and they never once forgive us for it."

It closes the catalogue on one finger, and for the first time and the only time it says a thing that is not a label, low, almost to itself. "There is one of us it has never managed to hang. One it cannot keep, has no likeness for, no rail, no number. You will not find that case down here; it stood empty before the first of these was ever filled." Then it opens the catalogue again and reads the next label, flat, and will say no more.

choices.label: Go on to the blank rail of the fresh case
Go on into the gallery proper

gated.aside: It reads your label in your own cadence, the one you taught it across a dozen houses, and you understand for the first time that a soul who carries everyone's next line may be the one most thoroughly without a label of its own.

blocks.s: You crouch by the child at the glass. Up close it is younger than you thought, an age you can almost set a number to, the age just before a thing, and its face has the unhurried frankness of a child that has never once had to get up into a morning it dreaded, because no morning has ever been let come to it.
"Is it real," the child says again, not to you exactly, to the glass, to the warm still figure behind it. It does not seem to want comforting. It seems to want to know.

You do not have an answer that is kind and true at the same time, so you give it none. The child turns the balloon's string in its fingers and presses its hand flatter to the warm pane. You have the plain sense, crouched there, that the child is a thing the gallery is keeping so well that it was never let to be let go, and that what that costs is not a thing the lit halls will tell you yet.

choices.label: Stay a moment, then go on into the gallery proper
Look in at the figure behind the glass

blocks.s: "Why count," you ask. "Five." He cracks the knuckle, eyes on the half-drawn glove. "Keeps my hands honest and my head off the work. A man sets the same hand long enough, he stops minding it's a hand. Six. Starts to think only of getting it true, truer, perfect, never mind whose it was. That's the bench talking. It found the part of me that likes a thing got exactly right and held there, and it gave me a hall full of things to get right. Seven."

He says the next to the figure, not to you. "So I count. While I'm counting I'm a man at a job and not the thing the work wants, which is the one who fixes you so true you're never let to move again." Then, dry, between numbers, a chin-jerk at the woman in the case: "Same gesture. Forty years. Eight." He sets the hand a hair's breadth over. "Still wrong."

choices.label: Ask him to let you on, deeper into the rooms
Leave him to his count and go to the blank rail

blocks.s: You walk a little way along the cases and let the gallery show you itself. The figures stand each in its own glass, each under its own green-shaded lamp, each caught in the middle of a gesture and held there: a hand half lifted to a hat, a head turned to a voice, a foot set to take a step it will not take. None of them is finishing anything. That is the thing you feel before you can name it, that nothing in here is being let to finish.

The brass rails run on under the cases in an unbroken line, label after label, a maker's mark and a city and a catalogue number and a name, the small print of a real trade applied to the keeping of people.

Far down the gallery a voice is reading the labels aloud, low and even, to no one, and it comes to the end of your own thought a half-beat before you do. Nearer, off a side opening, three rising notes are being hummed, and at the top they shake, just a little, and stop.

choices.label: Go toward the voice reading the labels
Go toward the humming
Go to the bright patch of floor and the attendant
Go on to the inner gallery door
Look into the glass-fronted cases along the near wall

blocks.s: You come to a fresh case, an empty one, the glass new-wiped and the plinth bare and clean, set ready for an acquisition not yet made. Under it the brass label-rail runs like all the others, and like all the others it carries a maker's mark and a city and a year and a catalogue number penciled soft in the slot, all of it filled in but the one place, the long blank slot where a name would slide, left empty, waiting.

The gallery wants a name in that slot. You can feel it want one, the patient lean of the murmur, the kind lamp angled just so over the bare plinth. It does not greatly mind whose. A name set in that rail would be kept, lit, labelled, looked at, never once let go.

You could say your own name here, aloud, your true one, the one that belongs to the person who is still real and can still walk out, and not the one the rail would give you. Or you could say nothing, and let the gallery keep its blank, and its patience, and its want.

choices.label: Say your own true name aloud at the blank rail
Say aloud that you are a visitor and not an exhibit
Hold your tongue and leave the slot blank

gated.aside: You read what the perfect label costs the figure under it: not a lie, exactly, just a person reduced to the one still pose a rail can hold, and held there, and called remembered.

blocks.s: You will not be kept quiet. You stand at the fresh case and you say your name again, and louder, all of it, your true name flung out into the warm, daring the gallery to deny it. The gallery does not deny it. The gallery does worse.

The warmth goes out of the halls like a held breath let go, all at once, and does not come back. The lamps burn on and give no heat; the floor is cold stone under a thin warm skin that is gone now; and the figures in their cases, every composed downturned face of them, turn, slowly, in their glass, toward the fresh case and the rail and you. From somewhere up above, in the dark over the lamps, a stillness fixes its whole attention on the one visitor who stood in a lit hall and shouted a true name. And the inner doors swing to, soft, all down the gallery, and the brass plates by them go dark, and the way on, the dark archway, the closed wing, the way down to the likeness you came for, seals against you, panel by panel, room by room.

You are right, is the terrible thing. You know exactly who you are. You stand at the foot of the fresh case with your true name in your mouth and the whole kept place gone cold and shut against you, marked, awake, certain, sealed out of the dark where the truth was, kept now not in the good light but barred from it, at the blank rail that would not hold your name, for as long as the gallery keeps its one stopped hour, which is as long as there is.

You say your name. Not loud. You say it to the blank rail, to yourself, the way you say a thing to be sure you still have it, the name from before you began to go out of people's minds, the name of a person who got older and lost things and walked on real streets where the clocks went round.

It is the strangest sound in the gallery. It does not echo; the warm takes it and does not give it back. For a moment the hall holds very still, the figures not-finishing their gestures, the murmur on a held swell, as if the whole kept place had turned its head at the one true word said in it. The blank slot in the rail stays blank. Your name will not go into it; you have said it as the living thing it is, and not as a label, and the rail cannot hold it.

Then the gallery comes on again, cooler toward you now by a degree it will not own to. You have your name in your mouth and cannot set it down, and the warm has gone off your shoulders, and you are marked here for what you are, the living original who can still walk out.

choices.label: Go down toward the place the floor is setting
Go to the period room and the figure at the case
Stand your ground and say it again, louder

blocks.s: You ask him to let you on, deeper, past the rope and down toward the end of the rooms. He stops counting. He turns his head and looks at you for the first time, and there is something behind the heavy face that is nearly relief, a man glad to use his hands to let a person by instead of setting one fast.

"Down." Nine. "You're sure. They mostly want to go up to the warm and the good lamps. The ones that ask to go on, I let go on." He unhooks a length of rope from a stanchion and stands it aside, his bulk between you and the open case the whole time, on purpose, you think. "Mind your step past the bench. And if you find the door out, you take it. I'm tired of getting hands true. Ten."

He hooks the rope back after you, soft, and goes back to his count, and the parlour with its heatless fire is behind you, and the way on lies open toward the far end where the floor is already, you can feel it, setting a place.

choices.label: Go on to the fresh case and the blank rail
Go down toward the place the floor is setting

blocks.s: You do not step onto the plinth. You keep your feet under you and your weight moving, and you make yourself walk on, past the kind lamp and the looped rope and the bare rail with its waiting blank, toward the dark at the end of the halls where the lamps give out.

The attendant's voice follows you, still gentle, a shade less warm. "You will tire of walking," it says. "They all come back to the plinth, in the end. It is here when you are ready." The warm floor is behind you. The murmur thins. Ahead, past the last green lamp, a low dark archway gives on rooms with no lamps lit in them at all, dust-sheeted shapes standing in the unlit dark, and the cold clean smell of a place the warmth has not been let into.

choices.label: Go on toward the dark archway
Look back once at the plinth you did not take

blocks.s: You hold your tongue. You came down to find a likeness, not to pick a quarrel with a brass rail, and there is a sense, standing at the fresh case in the warm, that a name said aloud here is a thing better not handled in a lit room. You leave the slot empty where it is, penciled all round with the small true print of the trade and blank in the one place that matters.

The gallery is easy with you for it. The warm stays close, the lamp stays kind, the murmur stays content; you have given it nothing to cool over. A docent's pencil hovers near the slot, ready, in no hurry. You did the quiet thing. You are not sure, going on, that the quiet thing left you any harder to keep.

choices.label: Go down toward the place the floor is setting
Go to the period room and the figure at the case
Go back to the label-lectern

blocks.s: The door does not know you. You put your hand flat to the panel and it stays a panel, shut, indifferent, a door for someone else. You are in the gallery on sufferance, walked in off the hall, but you are not yet written into the book that the inner rooms are kept by, and the gallery feels the lack of it, a guest under its roof who has not yet given a name.

From the bright lane behind you the attendant has come a few steps, holding out the warm floor again, and the murmur has thickened a little around you, patient, sure you will come round. There is a way to be admitted here without the long walk back to the desk. The gallery is always willing to take you on its books, if you will only stand a moment to be properly seen.

choices.label: Go and stand in the good light to be admitted properly
Go back toward the hall and the desk the long way

blocks.s: You keep your feet moving, past the attendant, past the kind lamp and the warm floor, the way you walk past a stranger holding a door who wants a thing for it. "I'm only passing through," you say, and saying it sets your feet more firmly under you; a visitor passes through, a visitor does not stand to be kept.

The attendant's smile holds and its eyes go flat, and it lets its hand fall. The warm floor is behind you. Ahead the gallery runs on, case after case, every figure in it once a visitor who stood in a good light for only a moment.

choices.label: Go on into the gallery proper
Go to the inner gallery door

blocks.s: You go back to the good light. You tell yourself it is only to rest, only to be warm a moment before the dark rooms, only to let the kind lamp take the cold off you, and you step into it, and the attendant is glad, and the murmur swells, cool and pleased, and you are looked at, and held, and it is enough, nearly, to stop you wanting anything else.

There was a thing you came down to face. You had the shape of it, in the great hall, a date, a turning face, someone who was letting you go. It is here somewhere still, you will take it up presently, when you are warm, when you are ready, in a while.

The while does not come. The good light is so kind and the looking is so steady and the shape of the loss goes soft at its edges and then goes, and you cannot, after a time, bring the face to mind, the one that was turning away, nor the date, nor why a person would walk down into cold dark rooms when they could stand here in the warm and be looked at and never let go. You stay where it is warm and you are wanted, and you forget the very forgetting you came down to face, which is the gallery's neatest trick of all, to keep you so well you lose the thing you came to keep.

You come to where the voice is reading. It comes from a figure in a docent's neat coat, stood at a small lectern between two cases with a slim catalogue open on one palm, reading the labels of the gallery aloud to no one, flat and even and unhurried, a tour for a tour-party that is not here.

It does not look up as you come. "And this," it says, reading, "is the likeness of one who came in out of the" and here it pauses exactly as long as it takes you to think the word cold "cold," it finishes, "some while ago, and was kept." It turns a leaf. "I have read this house so many times I have forgotten which gallery I first walked. You learn every label in it and lose the one room you started in. Funny, that."
It reads on, and the next label it reads is yours, your height, your coat, your way of standing, though it has not once raised its eyes to look at you.

choices.label: Stay and let it read your label to the end
Lean toward the label, let it know you, almost say yes
Go on past it to the blank rail of the fresh case
Go on into the gallery proper

blocks.s: You step off the marble into the low dark gap between two cases, out of the lamps, out of the eyeline of the figures, and you stand in it and watch the gallery instead of standing in the gallery to be watched. In the bright lane you were a thing being looked at; here in the unlit gap you are only a person, looking. You feel the difference at once, in your shoulders, in your breath.

The gallery does not like it. The warmth that had leaned toward you draws back a degree; the nearest lamps seem to brighten a fraction, as if to call you out into them. From the dark you can see the whole bright lane plainly, the cases and the kind lamps and the attendant at the warm floor, and you are no longer in any of it.

choices.label: Keep to the dark and go on down the gallery in it
Watch the glass-fronted cases from the dark
Go to the inner gallery door

blocks.s: You stand a while longer in the middle of the great hall and let the shape of the loss be near you. You do not look straight at it; you have learned, tonight, that the things in here are worst looked at straight, that the gallery wants your eye on the bright lit thing and not on the true one in the dark.

So you hold it at the edge of your sight, the date, the turning face, the someone who was, gently and without meaning unkindness, beginning to put you down the way a person puts down a book they mean to finish and never does. That is the forgetting you fled. Not a stranger's forgetting. A particular face's. The one face you could least bear to be let go by, going, by small degrees, and you unable to hold it, and the morning coming when it would be gone.

The murmur swells and settles. The figures look at nothing. You have the shape, and the shape is enough to be going on with, and the rest of the weight of it is waiting somewhere further in and further down, where the lamps give out.

choices.label: Go to the fresh case with the blank rail
Go to the period room and the figure at the case
Go down toward the place the floor is setting

blocks.s: You stay at the lectern, and the docent reads your label, and it is so nearly right, so nearly you, your height, your coat, your stoop, the cold you came in out of, the being-let-go you could not face, that something in you leans toward it the way you lean toward a stranger who has just said your name in a crowded room.

"They come back to be kept," it reads, flat, "and we keep them." And you find you want, very much, to be kept; to be the label read so exactly; to have a thing in this place know you to the last line. You almost say yes. You almost let the gallery finish you, set you, label you, hang you, the docent already a half-beat ahead of your own thought, finishing it for you.

But the gallery will not let the gesture finish, not yet, not even this one; the docent reaches the end of your label and turns the leaf and begins, flat, on the next figure's, and the warm holds you exactly where you are, leaning toward a yes the room will not quite let you say, for the next turn of the murmur, and the next, kept on the lip of being named, which is its own soft and patient kind of being kept.

63.title: The Girl Who Will Not Stand in It

blocks.s: You follow the humming to a side opening where the cases thin and an attendant has set up a clear bright patch under a particularly kind lamp, and a girl is stood at the edge of it with her arms folded, refusing to step in. She is young, and dry, and plainly still herself; nothing about her holds still, she fidgets, she picks a thread on her cuff, she keeps a small grubby tally penciled on the back of her hand where the lamps cannot read it.

"They keep offering me the good light," she says, by way of hello. "Tell you you look well in it. You do look well in it. That's the trick of the whole place. Nobody reads the labels but they'll all stand you under a nice lamp." She hums the three notes, low, and at the top they shake, and she catches them. "I'm not standing in it. I keep my own count and I keep my feet. You should keep your feet."
She will not come out into the bright lane. "Out there they can see you," she says, as if it were obvious, which it is.

choices.label: Ask her what the count on her hand is for
Keep to the dark edge with her and go on down
Leave her and go on into the gallery proper

blocks.s: You step out of the great hall into a period room set as a parlour: a fire laid and lit that throws light and no heat, chairs drawn up, a tea-tray put out, everything just so and waiting, a room dressed for a life that is not being lived in it. At one side, at an open case, a big man in a preparator's apron is at work on a figure, setting a hand a fraction truer, and he is counting under his breath as he works, low.

"One," he says, to the figure, not to you. "Two. Set the hand. Hold it." He cracks one knuckle. "Three." He has the build of a man who shifts heavy things and the slow care of a man who has done one close thing for a very long time. He does not look round. "You're not staff. Visitors keep the other side of the rope. Four."
The figure he is setting is a woman with a glove half drawn off, caught as if her name has just been said behind her, and her hand under his is warm.

choices.label: Ask him why he counts at work that never changes
Ask him to let you on, deeper into the rooms
Leave him to it and go to the blank rail

blocks.s: You step onto the low plinth and into the kind lamp and you let yourself be set. The attendant settles your shoulders and the docent's pencil goes to the rail, and you feel the gallery take a deep, glad, patient hold of you, deeper than the pose, deeper than anything tonight; this is the gallery getting its hands all the way round you, fixing you, giving you a place. It is peace. After the cold and the long way and the turning face that was letting you go, it is the purest peace you have felt, to be set, and labelled, and kept, and asked for nothing but to hold still.

Your weight goes easy. Your hand, half drawn from your pocket, stops where it is, and it would be no trouble at all, none, to leave it there, to leave all of you here, set, in the good light, never let go.

choices.label: Hold here a moment, it is so easy, it is everything you came for
Tear yourself off the plinth before the rail takes your name

blocks.s: "What's the count for," you ask. She turns her hand over and shows you the back of it, the penciled tally, grubby, gone over and over.

"Hours," she says. "I keep the hours. Out there a day's a day; you get up, it goes dark, you've spent one. In here it's always the same hour the clock decided on, and they want you to stop counting, because once you stop counting you stop minding that nothing moves, and once you stop minding that, you might as well be glass." She licks the pencil. "So I count. It's not much. It's the one thing in here that's mine and not theirs." Then, dry, nodding down the gallery: "There's a label fellow down there reads you off before you've thought it. Don't let him finish you. That's how they like you, finished."
The three notes come up again under her breath, and at the top they shake, before she stops them.

choices.label: Go down toward the label fellow
Go on into the gallery proper

gated.aside: She shows you the tally on the back of her hand and you find your own thumb has been keeping the same count against your forefinger, in the dark, without asking you, all night.

blocks.s: You come down to the far end of the lit halls, where the green lamps begin to thin and the cases give out, and here the gallery makes its real offer, in miniature, plainly, at last. There is a low plinth set ready under a particularly kind lamp, a velvet rope looped to one side, a brass rail beneath it with everything penciled in but a name. The attendant is there, and the docent, and the warm floor, all of it arranged, all of it patient, all of it for you.

"Here," the attendant says, gentle, hand out to the plinth. "You have walked a long way and faced a hard thing and you are tired. Stand here. Let yourself be set, just so, and labelled, and kept. You came so that you would never again be let go. This is where that is given. Hold this, hold it forever, and never fade and never change and never once be forgotten."
It is the whole of what you came for, held out on a warm plinth under a kind lamp, and the worst of it is how much, standing there with the shape of the loss still on you, you want it.

choices.label: Step onto the plinth and let yourself be set
Stay on your feet and keep moving toward the dark
Go to the dark archway past the last lamp

blocks.s: You go to the bright floor and let the attendant set you in the kind lamp, and the murmur swells and settles, and a slim figure in a docent's coat comes to the rail of the nearest case and writes something on the brass with a soft pencil, your height, your coat, your stoop, entering you the way a clerk enters an arrival.

"There," the attendant says. "Now you are with us, properly. Now the inner door will know you." A warmth leans in around your sleeve, and you understand you have been admitted, written down, taken onto the gallery's books, and that it cost you a turn in the good light to do it.

choices.label: Go back to the inner door, admitted now
Go on into the gallery proper

blocks.s: You step onto the warm floor and into the kind lamp, and you let your shoulders settle and hold the look a moment, only a moment, and let yourself be admired. The murmur swells, approving, cool and even, not a clap in it, only the mild steady attention of an eye that passes and is pleased. After so long going out of every mind, it is a great deal, to be looked at and to feel the looking hold.
The attendant makes a small contented sound. Your weight has gone easy. You take the pose once, and you feel it take a small hold in you, a thing the gallery now knows you can be asked to do again.

choices.label: Make yourself walk on, into the gallery proper
Hold the pose a moment longer, it is so easy
Step off and keep to the cases

blocks.s: You straighten and look in at the figure the child is watching. It is another child, behind the pane, on a low plinth, posed mid-play, a tin sailboat in its lifted hand and the red paint thumbed off the keel, the small scuffed shoe worn clean through at the toe the way a real child wears a real shoe. The label-rail under it gives a city and a year and a catalogue number, and the year is a long time ago.

The figure behind the glass and the child at your knee are the same child. One is kept. One is asking. You cannot, looking from the one to the other, work out which of them is the question and which the answer, and you decide, standing there, that you do not want to.

When you shift your weight the angle of the glass changes, and for a half-beat there is your own face in the pane instead, holding a museum stillness you do not feel, and then it is only your reflection again, tired, ordinary.

choices.label: Step back and go on into the gallery proper
Crouch again to the child at the glass
Stay at the case, looking, between the two children

blocks.s: You come to a long gallery of glass-fronted cases, the kind a museum stands its smaller, dearer things in, and behind the glass the figures are nearer to you here, close enough that your breath would fog the pane if your breath did anything. They are warm. You can feel it off the glass, the warmth of a thing a person has just left, though nothing in there has left and nothing has come.

At the foot of one case, on the visitor's side of the glass, there is a small figure. Smaller than the others, knee-high to the rail, stood very close to the pane with one flat hand against it, looking in at the figure behind the glass as though it were watching the sea. In the other hand, on a string, hangs the round shadow of a balloon.
The small mouth moves, the same shape twice, and you read it before you can stop yourself: is it real.

choices.label: Crouch and answer the child
Look in at the figure the child is looking at
Step back and go on into the gallery proper

blocks.s: You put your hand flat on the cool brass of the blank rail and you say it aloud, plainly, to the case and to the hall and to whatever keeps the hall: that you are a visitor. That you came in to look and not to be looked at. That you are not an exhibit and will not be a label, and that a museum is a place a person walks out of when the looking is done.

The words do not echo. The warm takes them flat. But you feel them set in you, true and a little dangerous, the way a thing is dangerous that cannot be unsaid. The blank slot stays blank; you have named yourself the wrong kind of thing for it, the kind that walks out, and the rail has no slot for that.

The gallery cools toward you. The murmur thins a moment, then picks up, patient. You are marked now, the one who said aloud that it is a visitor, which is to say the one who still knows the difference between coming to look and being kept to be looked at.

choices.label: Go down toward the place the floor is setting
Go to the period room and the figure at the case

blocks.s: You hold the pose a moment longer, and the moment is very wide, and time in it does not seem to be a thing that is passing. The attendant steps back to admire the whole effect, and the murmur swells, cool and even and pleased, and the kind lamp comes down over you and holds you in its small warm circle, and you let it.

You meant to walk on. You will, in a moment, when the look has had its fill of you. But the look does not have its fill; the look goes on, mild and steady, an eye that passes and is pleased and is replaced by another eye exactly as pleased, and you stand in the good light being looked at, and the standing is so much easier than the walking that you do not, after all, walk.

They bring a low plinth, in time, and a velvet rope, and a brass rail, and on the rail a name, not one you said, a kept one. Visitors will come, when the gallery opens to a morning you will not notice, and pause at you a moment, and not read the label, and move on, and you will be held in each cool look for that moment and let go again, held and let go, held and let go, forever, in the good light, never once let all the way go and never once anything but looked at. It is what you came for. That is the worst of it.

You look back once at the plinth you did not take. It stands under its kind lamp, low and warm and ready, the rope looped, the rail penciled and blank, and the attendant beside it with its patient hand still half out, sure of you, in no hurry. It would be the easiest thing in the world to go back to it. Every warm room you have walked tonight has been arranged to make going back the easy thing and going on the hard one.

The likeness you came to find is not out here on a warm plinth in a good light. It is in there, in the unlit rooms, behind the glass, where the gallery keeps the truth it does not put under a lamp. Or so you tell yourself, with the warm lamp at your back and the cold dark ahead.

choices.label: Turn your face to the dark and go on toward the archway
Go back to the warm plinth and the good light after all

blocks.s: You come to the closed inner door under its green lamp. It is a fine old door, panelled, with a brass plate beside it worn bright by a duster, and on the plate a line of small engraved letters: ADMITTED VISITORS BEYOND THIS POINT. By order. The door has no handle you can work from here; it opens to those the gallery has on its books and not to anyone else.

The commissionaire's voice reaches you from somewhere back toward the hall, mild, unhurried, without his coming. There is the small click first, a stanchion clip snapping to, and then: "You will have signed, going in. She does not open the inner rooms to a soul that is not on her books. Show the door you are admitted, and it will know you."

choices.label: Show the door your admission and go through
Go back and walk the cases first
Stand here a moment, not yet admitted, and listen

blocks.s: You hold there on the plinth, in the kind lamp, and you let the gallery's hold go all the way round you and in. It is the easiest thing you have ever done. You let your weight settle and your hand rest where it stopped and your face keep the ease it found, and the docent's pencil goes to the rail and writes, and you feel the name go in, not the one you would have said, a better one, a kept one.

The shape of the loss is still on you as it happens, the date, the turning face, the one who was letting you go, and the gallery's whole gift is that here, set, none of it can finish. The face cannot finish turning away. The morning cannot come. You will hold the loss exactly here, at this degree, forever, never let to feel it land and never let to set it down, lit and labelled and looked at, kept perfect and kept hollow.

Visitors will pause at you and not read the rail and move on, and you will be looked at and let go and looked at, and never fade, and never change, and never, ever be forgotten, which is what you came for, and which turns out to be a death you chose with your own two feet, in a good light, while a kind voice told you it was peace.

You come to the low dark archway past the last green lamp, where the lit halls end and the warmth gives out, and through it the rooms go on unlit, the cases gone to grey shapes under dust-sheets, the brass rails dark, the murmur thinned to a far far rumour of itself. It is colder through there, and cleaner, the camphor sharp, the wax gone faint. This is the back of the house, where the kept things wait off the lamps, and it is the way on, and the way down, toward the likeness of you and the choice you came to make.

On the wall beside the arch an old enamel sign hangs chipped at the corners: GALLERY CLOSES AT DUSK. Under it, on a bracket, a red fire-bucket full of sand, the sand swept up into one corner of it by a draught that has been working at it, patiently, a very long time. The sign is a small flat lie. The gallery does not close at dusk. The gallery does not close.
Through the arch the dust-sheeted dark waits, and somewhere far in it, where no lamp is lit, the thing you came down for is kept.

choices.label: Go through, into the dust-sheeted closed wing
Stand a moment at the threshold, between the warm and the dark

blocks.s: You come to the attendant and the clear bright patch under the kind lamp. It is the same offer the front hall made you, made again, more gently now, a host repeating an invitation to a guest who is plainly going to give in.

"Just here," the attendant says, hand out to the warm floor. "You held back, at the door. You needn't, you know. You came so far to be looked at; it is a small thing to let yourself be seen properly. Only stand a moment in the good light. The gallery does so like to see a visitor it can keep."
The floor is warm; you can feel it from where you stand. The lamp is kind. To step in and be admired would cost nothing and would feel, you already know, like being held in mind.

choices.label: Step into the good light and hold the look a moment
Keep your feet, stay a visitor passing through
Step past, into the dark service alcove instead

blocks.s: You go through the dark archway, out of the lit halls, into the dust-sheeted dark, and the gallery lets you go reluctantly, the warmth thinning at your back, the green lamps and the kind floor and the patient plinth falling away behind you, the murmur dropping to a far rumour and then to almost nothing at all.

The closed wing opens ahead and below, room into unlit room, the cases gone to grey humps under their sheets, the brass rails dark, the air cold and clean and sharp with camphor. No lamp is lit here. The figures wait off the lamps, sheeted, on their plinths, in the dark where the gallery keeps what it does not put under glass, and somewhere far in and far down, where no light reaches, is the likeness of who you stopped being, and the double that wears your coat, and the choice you came to make.

You have the shape of the loss now, the forgetting you fled and the one face it was going to take you from. You do not yet know what being kept will cost; that waits further in, in the cold, where the warm cannot follow. You take your weight off the last lit stone and go on, into the dark, into the closed wing behind the glass.
Mind the dust-sheets, going in. There is a great deal of dark ahead.

You stay at the glass-fronted case with the child, looking in at the kept thing behind the pane and out at the asking thing at your knee, and you do not go on. There is a warmth in it, a terrible warmth, in standing with the one child that is asking is it real and the other that is past asking, and you tell yourself you will go on in a moment, when you have understood it, when one of them has answered the other.

Neither does. The child at your knee asks, and the child behind the glass holds its tin boat in its scuffed shoe and does not answer, and the warm leans in around you, glad to have you stop, glad to have you settle here where the question is asked and never let to land.
