An attendant brings a low plinth, in time, for the visitor's side of the glass, and a rail, and a kind lamp, so that there are three of you now at the one case, two behind glass and one before it, all kept, all looked at, none of you ever let to finish the looking, and you forget, by warm degrees, that you came down to find anything at all but this.

The gallery opens out into a great hall, the largest you have come to yet, a long high room with the figures ranked down both sides in their cases under a hundred green lamps, and the murmur is loudest here, banked up under the high ceiling, a whole crowd's worth of low talk with no crowd anywhere in it. The figures all face a little inward and a little down, every one composed, every one looking at nothing, none of them at you.

You cross the floor of it, and somewhere about the middle, where the lamps are greenest and the murmur swells, it comes to you, the thing you came down to face and have been walking toward without looking at it. Not all of it. The shape of it. A date you have been not-counting. A face that was turning away from you, by degrees, the way a face turns from a window when the light goes. Someone was letting you go. Someone you could not bear to be let go by, and could not stop, and so you ran from the morning it was going to happen on, and came here, where nothing is ever let go.

You stop in the middle of the hall and the figures watch nothing and the murmur does not break, and you have it now, the shape of the loss, if not yet the whole weight of what it will cost to have it.

choices.label: Go to the fresh case with the blank rail
Go to the period room where someone is at a case
Go down toward the far end where the floor sets a place for you
Stand a while longer with the shape of it

gated.aside: The forgetting it shows you is the one you walked straight into last house, the slow loosening of every hold, and it offers you the cure you refused before: be kept so perfectly that no hold is ever needed, no one need remember you because the glass will.
A set figure turns its kept stillness toward you, wearing the very forever you chose last house, and the museum waits for you to recognise yourself in it and be comforted.
The label was left blank and waiting, it tells you, because you refused once and a refusal is only a thing not yet filed. The case has been held open. It has your measurements.

blocks.s: You come through the service door at the end of the long gallery, and the lit cases stop. Behind you the green lamps and the warm murmur; ahead, the closed wing, where the lamps are off and the figures wait on their plinths under dust-sheets, pale shapes standing in rows down a hall you cannot see the end of. The murmur does not follow you in. It thins at the threshold and falls away to a deeper hush, the hush of a room that has been shut a long time and means to stay shut.

The air is colder here, and drier, all camphor and old cloth and the faint mineral smell of wax kept out of the light. The sheets hang still. Not one of them stirs. Somewhere ahead is your own plinth, with your own label on the rail, and the likeness of you standing on it in the dark, and you mean to go and stand in front of it before the night is out.

You reach, without deciding to, for the nearest label-rail, to read whose figure waits under the cloth. The rail is dim and you have to put your fingers to it. The slot is empty. You take your hand back.

Deeper in, in the dark, someone is already speaking. It takes you a moment to place the voice, because it is your own, low and even and a half-beat ahead of you, saying the words you have not quite finished thinking, cool where your own throat is dry.

choices.label: Go straight toward the voice and your own plinth
Work down the dust-sheeted hall first
Step into the light of the one lamp still burning ahead

83.title: Why It Cannot Simply Take It

blocks.s: You ask why it does not simply take your place, if it is so good at being you. Why ask at all. Why not let you walk out tonight and stand here being kept without your leave.

"Because I can only ever be the copy of a thing that is," it says, and the half-beat is gone; it answers you straight, in your own plainest voice. "If I keep your place with you still walking the world, then I am you with you missing under me, and the looking would know it. Every eye that passed would be remembering a thing that is also out there being forgotten, and the remembrance would not run true. It would be a kept emptiness, not a kept you." It turns the ring, slow. "You have to give the remembrance over, on purpose, knowing what it is. Step up and be forgotten so the keeping is honestly mine; or walk out and be kept so the forgetting is honestly mine. The giving is the only thing I cannot do alone. It is the only thing I want, to stop being the thing that is only kept and to become, one way or the other, a thing that is. And it is the cruelest thing I could ask, because either arm erases one of us, and I am asking it kindly, which is the only way I was made to ask anything." Its eyes, your eyes, are dry; they did not give it tears. "Your walking out is the one thing that frees me from being only a copy. Your staying is the one thing that ends me, kept here remembered while you go on being. Pick which of us you can bear to do it to. Forgive me, or do not. Either is a thing I would get to have felt."

choices.label: Refuse both, but gently
Take the first arm; step up and walk out forgotten
Take the second arm; walk out and keep the plinth

blocks.s: You work down the closed hall in the dark, between the rows of sheeted figures. They stand on their low plinths under draped cloth, shapes you can read without wanting to, a shoulder here, a lifted arm there, a head bowed under its sheet. The cloths are old, washed soft, and one near you carries a faded laundry mark stamped in one corner and a neat darn where someone mended a tear a long time ago and meant the sheet to last.

The hush is total. Your own steps come back to you small. Down at the far end a dim doorway gives on a side store, and off to the side a short flight of stairs goes down into deeper dark, and partway along, a fresh attendant's clipboard hangs on a nail by a sheeted case, a pencil on a string beside it, the kind of ordinary thing a real museum leaves where the work is.

choices.label: Look into the side store
Mark the attendant's clipboard
Lift the corner of a small sheet you have noticed
Go to the sealed conservation door where a big man stands
Step into the dark alcove where someone is humming
Go on toward the voice and your plinth

85.title: Where the Child Comes Out from the Sheet

blocks.s: Further down the wing, where the rows of sheeted figures open at a cross-aisle by the head of the down stair, the dark is thinner, and there is more than one of you here now. The Ingenue is here, the new acquisition not yet set, still warm, still herself, standing clear of the nearest plinth with her chin up and her arms folded, refusing the cloth a porter has left draped half over her shoulder. She is humming, low, the same three rising notes, over and over, keeping them up between herself and the hush. And at the edge of the aisle, half out from under a small dust-sheet, there is a child. Small, still, a balloon held up in one hand on its string, the round of it turning slow. It is watching you. Its mouth makes the same small shape twice. Is it real.

The double has come soft-footed behind you, cool in your coat, and it crouches to the child's level over the dark floor. "Look at it," it murmurs to you, gentle, your face turned kind. "Step up and walk out, and the child keeps a you that never leaves, never ages, lit and remembered here beside it forever. Or stay, and let me go out, and the real you is here for it, kept, until the museum closes, which it does not. Either arm, the child is not left to watch your case go dark. The cruelty is the one where you walk out into the cold alone and leave it under its sheet with the place you were gone empty on the rail."

The Ingenue's three notes go up once and shake at the top, just a little, the sound she makes when she is frightened and will not show it, and she puts it down hard and rolls her eyes. "Worst part of being kept is the dusting," she says, to no one, to the wing. "They never tell you about the dusting. Forty years and a feather-duster twice a week, like you're a clock." Then, to you, dry and level: "Don't let it use the kid on you. It always uses the kid."

choices.label: Stand with the Ingenue and tell the child the truth
Let the double be the kind lie the child keeps

blocks.s: You come back along the dark wing to where a plain door stands in the end wall, no velvet rope, no brass rail, only a push-bar and a scuffed kick-plate, the way the figures came in and the cloth and the wax and the green-shaded lamps. The commissionaire is there, in his dark coat, who was not there a moment ago, a small mechanical click sounding from somewhere about him, a key turned in its ward, a half-beat before he speaks.

"You came out of your own plinth still yourself," he says. "Most do not. I close the sheet over them gentle and go back up for the next." The click again. "I will show you the thing I do not show the ones who have stepped up. This door is a staff door. It is how everything in here arrived, and a way a thing arrives is a way a thing leaves; it works both ways. Beyond it is the yard, and the cold street, and the ordinary world that keeps no one and lets everyone go." He sets his hand to the push-bar. "Mind, now. A body only finds the out of it while it is still moving, still its own, still unset. Stand too long under any lamp in here and the door is only a wall with a sign on it."

choices.label: Take hold of the door and feel that it opens outward
Take hold of the door and feel that it opens outward
Ask him the private thing he keeps off the catalogue
Reach for the bar without being shown it opens, and push

gated.aside: Your hand knows the cold of the staff door before your head decides anything. You could walk out of it as no one, into a street that will not keep your face, and some steady part of you has already started counting the steps down it.

You see what the door is for and what it costs: it is the only way out that does not end behind glass, and the price of it is written on every blank rail in the house, which is that you walk out into being forgotten and you do not get to keep even the forgetting.

blocks.s: You ask the shape of the offer. It reads it off a card it did not need to pick up.

"Two arms," it says, ahead of you the whole way, "and the thing on the rail between you is not a seat, and not a face, and not a part, and not the years you have left, whatever the doubles in the houses below other stairs have asked their visitors to hand over. It is your remembrance. Which of you is the one kept and which is the one forgotten." It turns a card. "One arm: take its place on the plinth, let the likeness be entered under your name, and walk out the staff door into your ordinary forgotten years, where no one keeps you and you leave no mark a glass will hold. The other arm: walk out yourself, and let it be the copy that goes out in your stead, and you keep the plinth, the label, the perfect remembrance, looked at forever." It sets the card down square. "Hear it out. Then do not hand your remembrance to a kindness. There. Said first, as I do."

choices.label: Ask it what it cannot file
Go on to your plinth and the one in your coat

blocks.s: You take the first arm. You tell it yes: I will step up, let the likeness be me, and walk out forgotten. Let me have the ordinary years.

It is gentle about it, because gentleness is what the house made it of. "Let me," it says, soft, and it comes down off the plinth at last and eases you up onto it in its place, settling your weight, easing your hand to the angle it has held so long, turning your collar under wrong because the wrong is the kept way now, sliding your ring onto the hand the rail expects. For a moment, stood below you in your skin, it is the gladdest thing in the wing, a copy about to stop being a copy by walking out into a life, and you cannot even grudge it, because you gave it freely and you saw what it was made wanting.

Then you are at the staff door, and out, into the cold yard and the wet street and a grey ordinary morning that takes you alive, and you have your years, every one, to age in and end in and be let go by everyone who ever held you. And it is real, the morning, and the years are real, and they are not quite yours, because back in the wing your name is on a rail above a perfect likeness, kept and lit and looked at, and on the worst mornings, ordinary and ageing and unremembered, you will feel it: a you still standing back there behind the glass, remembered flawlessly forever, that took your kept place gladly and would again, and you will live out every forgotten hour unable to say whether you were freed or only quietly erased from the one place that would have held you.

One of the sheeted shapes is small, knee-high to the plinths around it, and you have been not-looking at it for a while. You lift the corner of its cloth, only the corner. A child's shoe, the leather scuffed clean through at the toe the way a child wears out a shoe walking somewhere it did not want to go, on a small foot that does not move. Above the foot, where the cloth tents up, the round shadow of something on a string, held out into the dark.
You let the corner fall before you see the face. From under the sheet, very quietly, a voice no bigger than the shoe: "Is it real?"

You do not answer. You find you cannot yet. The small hand stays held out under its cloth, the balloon's shadow turning slow on its string, and you straighten up and step back into the aisle with the question following you down it.

choices.label: Go on toward the voice and your plinth
Look into the side store

blocks.s: You step into the lit bay, your bay, and the warmth comes up around you, and you let your weight settle on the bare plinth and your shoulders ease and your face find the museum calm, and it is the easiest thing you have ever done. The lamp comes down kind over you. No one had to trade you for the copy; you were not put in its place; you were given your own, which is better, which is the soft cleverness of it. You have a place of your own here now, lit and swept and yours.

They bring a rail, in time, and slide your name into the slot, the better kept name, and they bring no sheet, because you are to be seen. You kept the way out in your pocket the whole while; it is there still, cold, a stub of a street you will not walk, because why walk out into the forgotten dark when here you are admired, in your own good light, your own bay, never once let to fade. Visitors will pass, when the wing opens to a morning you will not notice, and pause, and not read the label, and move on, and you will be remembered in each look a moment and let go, held and let go, forever, kept perfect and hollow in a place that was made to feel like a gift, and that is the worst of it, that it felt like a gift, and that you took it gladly, and that it was.
You ask it what it is. It does not flinch; they did not put flinching in a likeness.

"A copy," it says. "The one the house took. You came in the night you could not bear to be let go, and the museum took the likeness of you and set it here, behind the glass, in your coat, and gave it your name on the rail, and stood it to wait for the night you would come back and find it. That is me. I am the you that was kept. I am very good at being you. I have had nothing else to do, standing here, but be it." It turns the ring on the wrong hand, slow. "Here is the part the labels leave off. The one who walks out has a life to be let go in. I have only the plinth and the coat and the looking. When you are not here there is nowhere I am; I am only looked at, and a thing only looked at is not quite a thing at all. I have been kept a long time, and I am so tired of being a thing that is only kept."
It is warm to look at and cool to be near, and not one part of it breathes.

choices.label: Ask what it wants, then
Tell it to take your coat off

blocks.s: You go to the child's side of the aisle, and the Ingenue comes with you, and you do not perform it, because a child hears a performance the way only a child can. You tell it small and plain: that the one in the coat is very good and very kind and is not you; that you are the real one, the warm one, still changing, still able to walk out; that being real and let go is worth more than a you that never leaves and never ages and is never quite there under the glass.

The child turns its whole self toward you, the balloon lowering. It does not ask its question again. The Ingenue crouches level with it and says, quite gently for her, that it is allowed to stop watching the door now, that nobody worth keeping ever liked being dusted anyway, and the child does something with its face that is almost the start of being a child again.

The double, behind you, makes a sound you have made yourself, the small catch before a hard line, and says nothing, because there is nothing in it that knows what to say to a thing this true. And the Ingenue looks at you properly then, head a little to one side, and you feel her see it: not the museum calm, not the cool copy, but you, the living one, warm and changing under the likeness.
"There you are," she says. "Under all of it. I see you. Good. Somebody saw it now; that's harder to hand into a case once it's been seen."

choices.label: Take the child's question with you and go down toward the workshop
You have not earned the way out yet; go and find the commissionaire
Stay in the hush here with the child and the lull

blocks.s: "What's the count for," you ask. She turns her hand over and shows you the penciled tally-marks, grubby, gone over and gone over.

"Days," she says. "I keep the days. Out there a day's a day; it gets dark, you've lost one and got a new one. In here it's always this same shut hour, lamps off, nothing moving, and they want you to stop counting, because the second you stop counting you stop minding that nothing changes, and the second you stop minding that, you might as well be set." She licks the pencil. "So I count. It's not much. It's the only thing in here that's mine and not on their books." The three notes come up under her breath, soft, and at the top they shake, just a little, before she catches them and stops. "Don't get set. Keep moving. It's the only thing that works."

choices.label: Go back to the dust-sheeted hall
Go on toward your plinth

gated.aside: She shows you the tally on the back of her hand and you find your own thumb has been keeping the same count against your forefinger, in the dark, without asking you, all night.

94.title: The Card That Will Not File

blocks.s: You ask it what, in all these drawers, it cannot file. The flat reading stops. It does not finish your thought this time; it reaches past it for one of its own, which it does so rarely that you hold still to hear it.

"There is one," it says, "that has no card. I have looked. Every soul this house ever hung has a card in here, a number, a rail, a name slid into a slot. One has none. It keeps every label and has no label. It files every one of us and is in no drawer. You will not find that case down here either; it stood empty before the first of these was ever set." It picks up the next card, and the flat tone closes back over the gap. "I should not have said. Go on to your plinth. It is waiting, and I have a great many cards."

choices.label: Go on to your plinth and the one in your coat

blocks.s: You leave the cross-aisle and the sheeted rows and go on, down, to the head of the stair the commissionaire's door does not lead to, the stair the whole closed wing leans toward, and you start down it. The dark deepens. The camphor and old cloth fall behind, and a colder smell comes up to meet you, plaster and cold wax and clay and the iron of tools, the smell of the place where the figures are made before they are ever set behind any glass.

You have refused the double, both arms of it, and it let you, turning the ring back the right way. You have the way out in your hand, the staff door that opens outward onto the cold street and the ordinary years, and you have not walked it, because there is a thing down below that set this whole exhibition, and you mean to look at it full in the face before you choose to be forgotten and free or kept and remembered. You take the down stair with the out of the door folded safe in your pocket like a stub of the waking world.

Behind you, in the cross-aisle, the child lets the balloon up off its string and watches it lift into the dark and not fall. The Ingenue keeps her chin up and will not take the cloth. The double stands cool in your coat at the head of the stair and lifts your hand, not a wave, only letting you see it let you go.

lines: THE CURATOR, close now in the cold below, and wistful, the way a keeper is wistful who has hung every soul and been hung by none:

Down to the workshop, then, with the way out still in your pocket. Good. The lit halls are where we keep you; the workshop is where we keep the keeping. You sent your double neither up nor out and kept your own remembrance on you, which I confess I always half hope a visitor will, since it makes the last offer land so much harder. Mind the stair; back of the glass it falls away from you faster than any gallery did. The keeper is at the catalogue, and your name is in it, and we have all the time there is, and all the remembrance, which is the one thing I keep for everyone and was never once given to keep for myself. A little while longer, then. Down you go.

blocks.s: Off the hall a heavy door stands in a brick partition, a conservation room behind it, and a big man has his shoulder set against it, holding it to. The preparator. He is in shirtsleeves, sleeves rolled, and he does not look round at you; he is counting, low, and between the numbers he cracks one knuckle.

"One," he says, to the door. "Two. It wants to seal. Three." The knuckle. "There is a master switch forward throws the case-glass down on every plinth in the wing at once, drops the lot, seals each soul under its own glass for good. The keeper has its hand near that switch. The day it throws it, the wing is final, and every figure this side of the sheets is kept under glass forever. Four. I hold this one off its seat, so there is a room back here the glass has not come down in. Five."

He sets his jaw against the brick. "You are back of the glass now. Go careful. The good quiet ones in here have stopped holding their doors. Mind which side of mine you are on when you come back. Six. Go on."

choices.label: Ask him what is past the conservation door
Go back to the dust-sheeted hall
Go on toward your plinth

97.title: Let Me Go Out in Your Stead (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the second arm. You will keep the plinth. You tell it: go out in my stead, wear my coat into the morning, live my forgotten years for me; I will stay, and be kept, and be remembered.

It does not argue, because the second arm lets it stop being only kept too, walking out into a life with your years on it. "Let me," it says, settling your ring onto its hand, your collar under wrong, dressing itself in your ordinary mortal life with a fond care that is the worst of it. "You never have to be let go again. You stay here, lit, labelled, looked at by every eye that ever passes, kept perfect when the living would have worn you to nothing. The forgetting is mine now. I carry it out for you." It thanks you. It means it. Then it is at the staff door and through it, your forgotten years on its back, and you step up onto the plinth in its place and let your weight settle and your hand find the held angle and your face take the museum calm.

And you stay. They draw no sheet over you; you are to be seen. The lamp will come on you when the wing is opened to a morning you will not notice, and visitors will pass, and pause, and not read the label, and move on, and you will be held in each look a moment and remembered, held and let go, held and let go, forever, kept flawless behind the glass while out in the running world a copy in your coat ages and ends and is forgotten in your name. It is what you came down for, to never be forgotten. You are not. You stand there remembered, perfectly, and hollow, scraped out to make the likeness the remembering is of, and that is the worst of it, that it is exactly what you wanted.

You stand a moment in the dark of the wing and let the commissionaire look at you, properly, the old courtesy thinning over the tired thing beneath. He takes his time. He has read a great many faces coming off his step and out from under his sheets, and been wrong about almost none of them.
"Hold still," he says, not unkindly. The click. "This is the part of the post that matters, and I do not do it for the ones already set, because there is nothing left under the wax to find."

He looks for the living thing beneath the museum calm. He looks for whether there is still a person in the coat who is cold, and changing, and bound for an ordinary morning. And his old face does the small upward thing of a man finding what he hoped to find. "There you are," he says. "Under the likeness. The warm one. The one still changing, that no rail has hold of yet. I see you, and now it is seen, and a thing once seen by another is harder to hand into the glass than you would credit. Go careful below. The keeper down there reads faces too, and is less kind about it than I am."

choices.label: Go on toward the child and the convergence
Pass the one bay where a lamp has come on over an empty plinth
Go down toward the workshop, the way out in hand

gated.aside: His clip clicks once and he holds your eye a beat, one doorkeeper to another, and does not say what you both know, that the ones who learn the door works both ways are the hardest to keep and the worst to lose.

blocks.s: You come to the foot of your own plinth and look up at the likeness of yourself, and it looks down at you. It is you, finished, perfect, cool and dry in the dim. It wears your coat. The collar is turned under on the wrong side, the side you have never once turned it. There is a ring on its hand that is your ring, and it is on the wrong hand. It stands the easy way you stand at the end of a long day, weight off one hip, and it is mouthing your words half a beat ahead of your thinking them, lips moving in the dark, dry, soundless, then sounding.

"There," it says, in your own voice gone nearly perfect. "You came down. Most do not. They take a good light upstairs and they never get back of the glass." It does not step down. It cannot; it is set. "I have stood here a long while, in your coat, being the you the museum took, the you that holds. Stand where you like. There is only the one plinth, and I am on it, and so it will have to be you below and me above, which is the whole of the matter, really, when you look at it."
The folded sheet lies on the floor between you. On the rail, your name, slid into the slot in the patient museum hand.

choices.label: Ask it what it is
Tell it to take your coat off and stop wearing your face
Tell it to make its offer plainly

blocks.s: "Two arms," the double says, settling on the plinth, both your faces in the leaked-down dark, the one warm and the one cool. "And I will say them plain, because you came down still moving, and a thing that still moves deserves plain.

"One. Take my place. Step up here, let the likeness be entered under your name, and let the rail say it is you. Then walk out the staff door, the way the figures came in, into the cold street and your ordinary years, and live them, ageing and ending and leaving no mark a glass will hold, and be let go by everyone who ever held you, forgotten, utterly, real and free and gone. And I get to be the kept one, the remembered one, looked at forever, which is the one thing I have ever been able to be.

"Two. Walk out yourself, tonight, as you. And I go out in your stead, the copy wearing your coat into the morning, and lives your ordinary forgotten years for you, ageing and ending out there, unremembered, while you keep this. The plinth. The label. The perfect remembrance, lit and looked at and never once let go, here, behind the glass, forever."

It folds your hands, the ring catching what light there is. "That is the trade. Not a seat to sink into. Not a face to hand over. Not a part to keep playing. Not the years you have left. It is your remembrance. Which of us is kept and which is forgotten. Both arms, one of us walks out into the forgotten years and one of us stays here remembered. The only thing on the rail is whose name the keeping goes under."

choices.label: Tell it you have heard offers in this shape before
Ask why it cannot simply take your place without you
Refuse both; you will not trade your remembrance away
Take the first arm; step up, let it be you, and walk out forgotten
Take the second arm; walk out, let it go in your stead, keep the plinth

gated.aside: It offers to be the one kept here, perfect and remembered, so that you can do again what you did last house, walk out into the losing; or to walk out itself wearing your forgotten years, so that you need never be let go. It thinks the second is mercy, after the bargain you made before. It half is.

It offers you the kept remembered place back, the stopped perfect forever you chose last house and wore like a good coat, and it cannot understand why your hand will not close on the thing your hand has always closed on.

You have let a thing be taken before and lived, so one arm of the offer reads to you almost as rest; but to be kept here is to take, not to let go, to hold your own face past its hour behind glass and never let it finish.

Hear the seam in it. Both halves end with you remembered and not real. One sends a copy out wearing your forgotten years; one keeps you behind glass wearing none. Neither is the walking out. Only the walking out is the door.

blocks.s: You step off the aisle into a dark alcove between two sheeted plinths, where the humming comes from, and the Ingenue is there, knees up, her back to a plinth, watching the hall with the frank unbothered look of someone who has decided she is not afraid and is mostly right. The cloth a porter draped on her lies kicked off in a heap. She will not come out where the one far lamp can reach.

"You're still warm," she says, by way of hello, looking at you properly, head on one side. "I can always tell. They go cool when they set. You haven't." She fidgets, picks at a thread on her cuff, keeps a small grubby count penciled on the back of her hand where the museum cannot read it. "There you are. Under the likeness they've got of you down the end there. Still moving, still changing. I see it. Somebody should, before you go and decide anything; it's harder to hand yourself into a case once you've been seen out of one."

choices.label: Ask her about the count on her hand
Go back to the dust-sheeted hall
Go on toward your plinth

blocks.s: The side store is a long narrow room of shallow drawers and pigeonholes, every one stuffed with brass label-rails and slips and old catalogue cards gone soft at the corners from handling. A workbench runs down one wall. And at the bench, in a docent's neat coat, a figure stands turning the cards over, reading them to itself, flat and low, a tour given to a room of names with no faces.

It does not look up. "You will have come down to your own plinth," it says, reaching the end of your thought a half-beat early, "the way they all do, the ones who get this far," and it sets down a card. "It is waiting for you, in your coat, and it will make you an offer in two arms, and I can tell you the shape of it before you hear it, because I have read the labels of everyone who took it and everyone who did not, and I cannot help knowing which card goes where."

choices.label: Ask it the shape of the offer
Ask it what it cannot file
Leave it to its cards and go on to your plinth

blocks.s: You tell it to take the coat off. Your coat, with the collar it has turned under wrong and your ring it has put on the wrong hand. To stop wearing your tired face at you in the dark.
It does not get angry; they did not put anger in it either. It only looks down at you, patient, and turns the ring a fraction, deliberately, seeing how much it is allowed.

"I cannot take it off down to anything," it says, gently. "There is no me under the coat. You are the only one of us that undresses back to a person. That is the whole of what we have to settle, and you standing below me does not move it; it only sets a crick in my neck, looking down, and I have your neck now, see." It rolls its shoulder, your exact small wince. "Stand if you like. But you will want to hear it, because one arm of what I am going to say keeps you remembered forever, and you came down a long way into the dark to keep from being forgotten."

choices.label: Hear the offer

104.title: The Kind Lie Under the Sheet (an ending)

blocks.s: You let it stand. You tell yourself the double is right, that the kindness is the lie, that the child should keep a you that never leaves and never ages, here in the safe lit hush, and that the cruelty would be walking out alone and leaving it under its sheet to watch your case go dark.

The double rises and crosses to the child and crouches, in your face, your coat, and says the warm true-sounding thing you would not refuse it the right to say. The child looks up at the loved face, the one that will never leave, and is comforted, and does not ask its question again, because it has been given an answer, and the answer is a beautiful lie, and a child cannot tell a beautiful lie from the truth, which is most of what it is to be a child, and the house knows it.

The Ingenue says your name once, sharp, from across the aisle, and you do not turn, and after a moment you hear her humming stop, because there is no use humming at a person who has decided to be kept. You step back to your own plinth to let the double and the child have the moment, and the hush closes over you, cool and dim and flat, because a person who believes the loved lie is kinder than the cold truth does not, in the end, walk out the staff door. You stay to watch them be safe. You stay. The balloon drifts up off its string into the dark and does not fall, and no one reaches to bring it down, and the morning does not come to open the wing, which is the only mercy the hush has, and it gives it to everyone, and it is no mercy at all.

You take the clipboard down off its nail. It is a real thing, board worn pale where a thumb has held it, a sheaf of accession slips clipped to it, the top one half filled in a careful hand: a number, a date, a column headed disposition left blank. A pencil swings on its string. It is the same hand as the visitor's book in the front hall, the same patient museum hand that writes everyone down.

You could put yourself on it. There is a line for it, a place to enter, the small clerkly act that says the house has you and means to keep you. You take the pencil. The lead drags and leaves nothing, and you lift it; then the graphite catches, after all, and your entry sits there grey among the rest, on the books of the wing now as you were on the books of the hall.

choices.label: Hang the clipboard back and go on to your plinth
Look into the side store first

blocks.s: You set your hand to the push-bar and lean, and the door gives. Cold air comes in at the gap, real cold, street cold, with the wet-stone smell of a yard and a city beyond it, and a bar of grey from a lamp out on the pavement falls across the kick-plate. The door opens outward. It is real. It is a road, and it runs both ways, and you came in by something like it and you could go out by this one now, into the cold and the ordinary night and the years that keep no one.

You hold it a moment on its hinge, the cold on your face, the wing's hush at your back. Then you let it ease to, not shut, only to, because you are not done; there is a thing down below this wing that set the whole exhibition, and you mean to look at it full in the face before you choose the cold street or the glass. But you have the way out now. You know it for true, the out of it, in your hand, and the wing cannot tell you again that the only way left is up onto the plinth.
The commissionaire watches you let it to, and something behind his eyes settles. "There," he says. "You have it now. Mind you do not set it down."

choices.label: Let the commissionaire look at you properly before you go on
Go on toward the child and the convergence
Go straight down toward the workshop, the way out in hand

blocks.s: One lamp is still on, down a side bay, green-shaded like the others, throwing a single warm pool onto a fresh case where a new figure has been set and not yet sheeted. A woman, finished and perfect, mid-turn, one hand lifted to a hat she will never finish setting. The light is good. It is the only good light in the wing, and it knows it.

There is a clear patch of floor beside her, swept, waiting, the way a place is kept beside a portrait for the sitter to stand. You could step into it. The lamp would take you too. After the cold dark of the threshold the warmth of the pool reaches out at you, and it would cost nothing to stand in it a moment and be lit.
Or you keep to the dark edge of the bay, out of the pool, and stay a thing the lamp does not have, and go on.

choices.label: Step into the lit pool and let it hold you a moment
Keep to the dark and stay out of the light

blocks.s: You keep to the dark edge of the bay, skirting the lit pool, and you do not let the lamp have you. The new figure stands in her good light with her hand to her hat, and you go by her in the cold, a person passing, not a thing lit and kept. Staying out of the one light in the wing takes a small deliberate effort; the warmth is right there, and you walk past it.

The lamp does not dim, exactly, but the pool of it seems to lean after you a degree as you pass, reaching, not quite letting you go. You keep to the dark, out of its eyeline, a watcher and not a watched thing, and you keep moving. A thing that keeps moving cannot be set.

choices.label: Go on toward the voice and your plinth
Work down the dust-sheeted hall

blocks.s: You say no. Not to be cruel; you find you are not being cruel, only clear. You did not come down the whole dark length of this wing to settle your remembrance by stepping into the glass forgotten or by buying a kept place forever at the cost of a copy sent out to be forgotten in your name. To take the first arm is to walk out alive and ordinary on the back of a likeness entered under your name forever. To take the second is to be kept and remembered by handing your one mortal life to a copy and letting it carry your forgetting out into the morning. Both arms keep one of you and erase the other. You will not make the trade on those terms, for yourself, or, you find to your own surprise, for the cool dry thing standing in your coat.

The double does not argue. It nods, your nod, slow. "I knew you might," it says, quietly. "I hoped you would not, and I knew you might, and that is the worst of being made out of you. I can see exactly why a person would refuse, and the refusing leaves me standing here, kept and not, forever." It turns the ring back onto the right hand, the first true thing it has done, and reaches up and turns the collar down on both sides, the way you turn it. "Go on, then. There is a way out of this wing that is not the plinth. The commissionaire is by the staff door. He will show you it works both ways, if you are still moving enough to walk it. And the child is down the hall; I would have used the child on you, if you had wavered, because it is one of the levers I was made with. I am glad you did not waver. Go."

choices.label: Go and find the commissionaire and the staff door

blocks.s: You ask him the thing he keeps that the catalogue does not list, the one private thing. The click comes, a watch-case shutting, and for a moment the smooth old courtesy thins over something tired beneath it.

"A cloakroom ticket," he says. "Not a passenger's. A child's, off an old admission, a half-fare with the name worn off, that I never matched to a coat and never threw out, because I could not bring myself to keep it or to let it go." He folds his hands on it where it sits in his breast pocket. "A man who has admitted the company to every exhibition this house ever hung ought to have one soul he never wrote down. I keep the question of it where she cannot read it." The click. "That is mine, and now you have had it, and you will not have it again. The door, when you are ready. It opens outward. I do not show that to a soul who has stepped up onto a plinth, because to one of those an open door is only a crueller wall. You are still moving. So."

choices.label: Take hold of the door and feel that it opens outward
Take hold of the door and feel that it opens outward

blocks.s: On your way along the wing a lamp has come on, green-shaded, over an empty plinth in a clean swept bay, the only lit place between you and the stair. The bay is yours; you understand it without being told, the way you understand the look of a place laid for you. No glass yet. No sheet. Only the warm pool and the bare plinth and a rail at its foot with a slot ready for a name, your name, the better kept one, not the grey entry you penciled on the clipboard.

It is not the plinth with the copy on it. That one was settled, finished, behind you in the dark. This one is empty, and lit, and waiting, and the difference is the whole of the temptation: you would not be taking the copy's place, you would be having a place of your own, here, admired, in your own good light, kept perfect and never let to fade.
The staff door is cold in your pocket. The stair down is darker still. The lit bay is warm and it is asking nothing but that you stand in it.

choices.label: Step into your own lit bay and let them keep you well
Leave the lamp and go on toward the convergence
Leave the lamp and go down toward the workshop

blocks.s: You step into the lit pool beside the new figure, and the warmth comes up around you, and for a breath you let it. It is the first warm thing since the threshold. The lamp lays its green-gold on your hands and your coat and the side of your face, and you stand a moment beside the woman with her hand to her hat, two figures in one good light, and the wing seems to hold its hush a little kinder for it.

You take the pose only for a moment. But the floor is warm under you, and the lamp is steady, and you can feel how a moment becomes a posture and a posture becomes a place. You make yourself ease your weight back onto your own feet before it sets.

choices.label: Step back out of the pool and go on
Work down the dust-sheeted hall instead

113.title: Reaching for a Door You Never Earned (an ending)

blocks.s: You will not wait to be shown the out of anything. There is a push-bar under your hand and a cold edge of grey at the jamb, a way off this kept floor, and you throw your weight on the bar without the commissionaire, without staying moving, without first being shown that the door runs both ways.

The commissionaire says your name once, behind you, not sharp, only sorry, the click not coming because there is no key to turn for this. The bar gives, and the door swings, and beyond it is not the yard and not the street but more wing, another dark hall of sheeted figures running away into the camphor, and you go through it fast, and through the next, hunting the cold edge of grey that is always one door on, and the staff door that should open outward only ever opens onto more inward, because you came at it having let the moving go out of you, and to a thing that has stopped moving every door in the house is a wall with a sign on it. The dust-sheets crowd closer. The lamps fail to one in three and then to none. Somewhere a call-bell rings, flat and small in the dead hush, summoning no one, and you go toward it because it is the only sound, deeper and deeper into the closed wing, until you are only one more pale shape standing still in a row of them, under a cloth you do not remember drawing over yourself, in a hall with no door out that you, having stopped, could ever find.

"What is past it." Seven. He does not turn his head. "The stair down. The workshop, the moulds, the catalogue, the one who set this exhibition. Eight. You will be wanting that way, if you came down here still moving and not to step up onto a plinth in a good light. The doors above this one come down soft. This is the last glass a man can still hold off by hand."
He cracks the knuckle and braces his boot.

"I will tell you what I tell the rest. Find the commissionaire before you go deeper. Nine. He works the staff door, and there is a thing he can show you the rest of us cannot, whether a way that comes in goes out as well. A man should know the door runs both ways before he takes the stair. Ten." The count breaks off. He shoves the door back a hand's-breadth and holds it, spent. "Go and find him. I will keep the glass off this room."

choices.label: Go on toward your plinth and the one in your coat
Look into the side store first

115.title: Not a Seat, Not a Face, Not a Part, Not the Years

blocks.s: You tell it you have heard offers in this shape before. A chair to sink into and stop. A face on a hook, handed over so you never have to choose. A part to go on playing, perfectly, to applause. The hours you had left, and which of you would spend them. You know the look of a thing in your coat that wants what you carry.

The double tilts its head, your tilt, and for once it does not reach ahead for your words. "A seat," it says, turning them over like coins it was not struck from. "A face. A part. The years. Yes. I have heard of those houses." It almost smiles. "This is none of them. I do not want you to sit and stop; the plinth is for standing. I do not want your face for a mask; I have your face already, worn a little wrong, and it is no use to me off you. There is no part down here, the applause is two halls back. And it is not your time; your hours are your own, spend them how you like. What I am asking for is your remembrance. Whether you are the one this house keeps, lit and labelled forever, or the one who walks out into the dark to be let go by every mind that held you." The half-beat falters. "A seat is loneliness you can rise from. A face is a mask you can lift. A part ends when the run ends. Time, at least, you are spending either way. But to be forgotten is a thing that cannot be taken back once it is given, and to be kept is a thing you cannot lay down once you are set. I thought you should hear it named, because you came down still moving, and a person still able to walk out deserves to know exactly what it is they are being asked to be kept from, or kept as."

choices.label: Refuse both; you will not be traded into the glass
Ask why it cannot simply take your place alone
Take the first arm; step up and walk out forgotten
Take the second arm; walk out and keep the plinth

blocks.s: You go on down the centre of the wing, toward the voice that has your voice, and the sheeted figures fall away behind you on their plinths. The voice does not call you. It only goes on, low and even, finishing the ends of thoughts you have not reached yet, settled and certain, having heard you come the whole way.

The aisle ends at a plinth set a little apart from the rows, the only one in the wing with its sheet drawn off and folded on the floor beside it. A label-rail stands at its foot, and in the slot, your name. Above the rail, on the plinth, lit by no lamp at all, only by what little light leaks down the hall, stands a figure in your coat.

You stop a few feet off. You came here to do exactly this, to stand in front of it; and now that you are here you find your hand has gone, without your asking, halfway to your own collar, to check it is turned the way you turn it.

choices.label: Go and stand at the foot of your own plinth

117.title: Let the Hush Set You (an ending)

blocks.s: You saw the child, and you told it the truth, and you were seen for the living thing you are, and then the hush comes up around you all the same, dim and dry and restful in the way the worst of this wing is restful, and you find you are tired, the long-way-down tired, and the dark at the edge of the aisle is not cold any more so much as easeful, and the small effort of staying moving, the one the commissionaire warned you to keep, slackens, and you do not take it back up.

The Ingenue keeps her chin up beside you, and keeps it up, and says, keep moving, walk a step, any step, and you mean to, you do mean to, and the sheeted figures stand patient all down the dark and the one far lamp burns green over its fresh case and the next step does not come. You stop being sure how long you have stood. You stop minding that you have stopped. The child watches you go still with the same patience it watches everything, and the double, cool in your coat, does not even need to make its offer now, because you have given it the only thing it wanted without being asked, your moving, your changing, your one chance to walk out, let go of into the dry hush that keeps everything. A porter comes, in time, and draws a sheet up over your shoulder, and you do not shrug it off, and the laundry mark in its corner sits faded against your coat, and you stand there set, eyes open, kept, among the covered figures, for as long as the hush will last, which is to say until the museum lets a single thing go, which is to say never.

The last stair lets you off into the cold, and the cold here is a working cold, the cold of a room where heat was never wanted, where the wax must stay hard and the moulds must keep their faces. The workshop runs away from you under one low strip of bulbs, dim after the green lamps above: racks of moulds along the walls, plaster faces turned inward to the wall so that what you see is the blank backs of them, rank on rank, each one the negative of a person who is upstairs now behind glass. The wax-pots stand on their burners gone cold and skinned grey, the burners dead, the wax in them set hard months or years ago and never poured.

You came down with the way out in your hand, the staff door the commissionaire showed you above, and you have it still. This is the bottom of the lit part of the museum. Whatever is true here is true in the cold and not in the warm.

In the middle of the floor stands the preparator's bench, dead-still, and on a stand beyond it the great catalogue lies open with the accession ledger beside it. Somewhere above, on the gallery that overhangs this room, a pen scratches once across a page, slow, the sound of a verdict being noted before the one judged has even been looked at, and then a chair eases, and then, deliberate, a single slow clap.

lines: THE KEEPER, dry, from the dark of the gallery above, before he troubles to descend:

You came all the way down. They mostly stop in the warm; the warm is built to make stopping feel like arriving. But you came down to the cold, where the work is done. Good. I keep the catalogue. Come to the bench, and I will tell you what you are, which is a thing no one has bothered to do for you in a long while.

choices.label: Go straight to the bench and the voice above
Work the mould-store along the wall first
Cross to the catalogue stand and the ledger

blocks.s: At the dark edge of the room there is a door, a plain working door, painted institutional green, a push-bar across it and a small enamel sign screwed above: STAFF ONLY. It is the twin of the one the commissionaire showed you above, the way out that works both ways. You put your hand to the bar. It gives. Beyond it a draught comes up cold and real off bare stone, an outside cold, a street cold, the cold of a world where things are lost and let go.

You let the door fall to again, not yet, but you have it now, here, in this room: the way out is at your back and you can reach it. You came down with it in your hand and now you have laid your hand on it. Whatever the Keeper says at the bench, you say it knowing the door is here.

choices.label: Go to the bench and the Keeper, the door at your back
Go to the catalogue stand first
Step through now, leave before you hear the rest

gated.aside: Your hand knows the cold of the staff door before your head decides anything. You could walk out of it as no one, into a street that will not keep your face, and some steady part of you has already started counting the steps down it.

You see what the door is for and what it costs: it is the only way out that does not end behind glass, and the price of it is written on every blank rail in the house, which is that you walk out into being forgotten and you do not get to keep even the forgetting.

blocks.s: You will not be read. You tell him so: that you did not come the whole long way down to have a man with a pen tell you what you are worth, that you came for a likeness and a person and not for a verdict, that he can keep his judgement.
He does not raise his voice. He picks the pen back up.

"You mistake the post," he says, dry. "I do not read you to your face for sport. I read you because a soul unjudged cannot be entered in the catalogue, and a soul unentered drifts about the workshop getting into the moulds. You may refuse the verdict. You may not refuse to have one. An idle hand fills a blank, and my hand is on the pen, and the blank beside your name is the one entry in this whole book that has gone unfilled, this hanging and the last and the one before." He looks at you with the flat patience of a thing that has heard every refusal a frightened soul can make. "Stand and hear what you are weighing, and go down to the vault knowing it. Or refuse, and I will mark you for what refusing makes you, and keep you for it, which is a commoner and a duller verdict than you would care for."

choices.label: Stand, and hear what being kept cost
Keep refusing; let the pen do what it will
Refuse, but you are awake and moving; hold his eye

121.title: The Glass That Came Down on Her (an ending)

blocks.s: You came down without the thing that would have let you help her. No one saw the living, changing you under the museum-calm and named it; you found no staff door to put at your back; you handed those away somewhere above, to a kind lamp or a good chair or a warm hour, and so when you put your shoulder under the descending glass beside hers the bell does not give the half-inch a soul gets when the house has had to admit it cannot quite keep one. It only comes down, slow, certain, an inch and an inch, and the two of you cannot check it, and you both know it before either of you says so.

"It's all right," she says, which is the worst thing she could say, because she does not mean it and she is saying it for you. "You tried. Most don't even try. Get your hands clear, go on, I'll not have it take your hands as well." The three notes, low, no shake left in them, which is worse than the shake. The glass grinds the last of the gap shut over her, her face going to a line and then to the kept museum-calm, and you hear the count come up the floor, the preparator letting the chain go because the house had her measure and you had nothing to add to the scale.

You go down to the vault the way you came, with the way out still in your hand, but lighter by something you will feel the lack of in the dark: the proof that a person can stay real, that someone stayed unset tonight. She did not. You were the one who could have weighed the scale and you came down without the weight, and you will carry her three notes down into the deep, low and unshaking and shut away under glass, the whole long way to the choice.

You follow the three rising notes to the empty plinth in the middle of the floor, where a great case-glass hangs slung on chains above it, a bell of glass meant to come down over a finished figure and seal it for good, and it is coming down, slow, a hand's width at a time, paid out chain-link by chain-link from somewhere off in the dark. The girl is under the lip of it, the newest in the house, in the first-night frock too good for her, and she has her shoulders braced up against the descending rim and her heels on the cold floor, holding it, and she is humming, three notes, rising, shaking at the top.

"Oh, wonderful," she says, through her teeth, not stopping pushing. "You. I don't suppose you came down with anything useful this time either. They've started lowering it on me. Funny thing, they only ever start lowering the glass once you've nearly worked out how to leave the room." The glass grinds down another inch and she swears, cheerful, terrified. "I kept my count, you know. The whole way down. Penciled where the house couldn't read it. I just need to get out from under this rotten bell before someone lets the last of the chain go and I'm set for the duration, which is forever, which is a long hanging."

choices.label: Get your shoulder under the glass beside her and free her
Get your shoulder under it; someone saw the real you, use it
You have no way to help; the glass is too heavy

blocks.s: He leads you behind the racked moulds to where the cold is oldest, a low bay between the wall and the last of the shelves, and sets the lamp on a ledge so its one reach of yellow falls into the bay and no further.
In that reach, on a low stool because the legs do not reach the floor, there is a child.

You knew there would be. You have caught the edge of it the whole way down, the small shape under the dust-sheet, the scuffed shoe, the question read off a frozen mouth through glass. Knowing did not ready you. The child sits very still in the cold, not crying, not lost, only still, the stillness of a thing that was never wound. A likeness kept perfect at an age, a young age, the age before the forgetting, the age its keeper could not bear to let change. A foil balloon is tied to the stool beside it, gone dull and slack, and it drifts low in the cold and taps, once, against the iron leg.
The brass rail under the stool gives a name, and a date set, and the date is older than any of the moulds. This one has been kept the longest of all.
The Keeper lowers the lamp and, for once, says nothing, and lets the cold do the work.

choices.label: Go to the child
Ask the Keeper what it is
You cannot. Turn back rather than feel this

blocks.s: You come back out of the bay, and you are not the same coming out as you went in, and the Keeper sees it, and marks it, and does not gloat. He sets the lamp on the bench.

"Now you know what you are weighing," he says, and the dryness has gone thin enough to see through. "That is the cost named at last, and not in a sentence, because I told you it could not be. To stay in this house is to be kept like that: held perfect, remembered, looked at, witnessed by every eye that passes, and never once let to change or age or fade or be let go. That is real. It is not nothing. A great many choose it, and they are not fools, and they are not cowards. To be remembered forever is a thing most of the living would give an arm for."
He turns toward the empty plinth under its slung glass, where the preparator counts.

"And to leave is to be the opposite of that child: to walk out into the street and the years and let yourself be forgotten utterly, no likeness, no rail, no entry, gone from every mind that ever held you, including the one that loved you enough to build this. Real, mortal, changing, free, and let go for good. That is real too, and it is a true surrender; do not let anyone tell you it is only freedom. I will not tell you which is the braver. The post is only to make you go down to the vault knowing both. The way down is past the glass. Mind the preparator. His hand is on the chains."

choices.label: Go to the glass and the preparator, the way down
Go first to the girl at the glass
You have seen it whole, and you cannot face being forgotten; stay to be kept

blocks.s: You go past him to the last stair, and the preparator holds the glass, his hand off the cleat and his count held still, and below you the air changes, colder, older, a deep-storage cold with no lamp in it at all, the dark where the house keeps the things it does not show, and somewhere down in it, faint, is the original mould and the one blank case and the choice the deep dark is at last allowed to ask.

You are not the one who came down the stair into the workshop an hour ago. You came down with the way out in your hand and you have it still, and now you have the rest of it too: the Keeper's verdict, that what the museum finds worthy it keeps behind glass forever and strikes the rest from the catalogue, and that to be let out the door you must be willing to be forgotten; the cost witnessed and named in full, the child kept perfect at the back of the cold, the likeness that outlived the person, never let to fade or change or be let go; the struck entry in the ledger, the keeper who is in no catalogue, the one case that was never filled, planted in you now and not explained. You go down knowing all of it, which does not make the choice below easier. It makes it true.

Behind and above, the Keeper's lamp is a small steady point at the bench, and the pen does not move, and you do not know, and may never know, whether he filled your verdict in or struck it through. From the warm far above, faint, a single slow clap comes once and stops.

lines: THE CURATOR, close now, and for the first time not entirely steady, rising up the last of the dark to meet you:

Down to the vault at last, where every visitor must finally stand alone before the glass. You went and you looked at it; I felt you do it; almost no one does, and it changes the cold. I will not pretend I am glad. The ones who look are the hardest to keep and the hardest to let go, and I was never any good at the letting go. I kept a likeness once, you know, because I could not bear it to fade; I never did manage to be kept myself; that is the whole of what I am and the one thing I may not say. Go down. The blank case is waiting, and so, for as long as you like, so am I, who have rather too much of the for-as-long-as-you-like, and no case of my own to be set in.

blocks.s: The stair takes you down toward the deep dark, and the original mould, and the choice the cold below is at last allowed to ask.

You went into the cold bay and you looked at the child kept perfect at the back of the dark, and you took the small hand, and you came out knowing the whole of it, the way you know a grief by having had it and not by being told. And knowing it, clear-eyed, with the cost named and no part of you fooled, you find you cannot do the other thing. You cannot walk out the staff door into the street and the years and let yourself be forgotten utterly, gone from every mind, no likeness, no rail, the one who loved you enough to build this left with nothing at all of you. You have seen what being forgotten looks like from the inside of the one who could not bear it. You will not do it to them.

You tell the preparator to leave the glass be. Not to seal you, and not to make you choose, only to let you be where you are. You go back, past the racked moulds, to the cold bay, and you sit down on the cold floor beside the child that was never let to fade, and you take its hand, and you stay.

It is not a defeat, exactly, and it is not peace, exactly. You have done a clear thing with your eyes open: chosen to be kept, because you have seen forgetting whole and judged you cannot do that to the one who built you a house against it. The child does not change; you did not promise it would. The balloon drifts low and taps the iron, dull, once. Above, the murmur turns over the same crowd it never quite gathers, and the count goes on at the bench, and you sit in the workshop's cold quiet with the one kept thing you would not leave alone in the dark, remembered, witnessing, never to fade, where nothing more will ever change you because you have sat down at last in the place where nothing is ever let go.

You put your shoulder under the cold rim beside hers and you heave, and the two of you together check it, just, the glass grinding to a stop a hand's width off the plinth and no lower, and you both stand braced under it, hearing the chain creak overhead and the preparator counting at the bench, one, two, his hand on the pay-out cleat, fighting himself.

"Right," she says, breathing hard, all the armour off it now. "Right. On three you take the weight a half-beat and I roll out under the lip, and you keep your side up so it doesn't take my hand. You've that look, you know. The look that hasn't been set yet. The look that's still its own."

You have, in fact, been seen, or you have the way out in your hand, and either way the glass knows it, and gives the half-inch a soul gets when the house has had to admit it cannot quite keep one. You take the weight. She rolls clear, scrambling, and comes up on the far side, lit by the bench-lamp, entirely her own size, no museum-calm on her at all.

"There," she says, and then, recovering, dry: "God, that was close. Best I've ever felt." She does not thank you, which is the highest thing she could do. "A person got out tonight. Stayed unset. Keep that; you'll want it where you're going, down in the vault. Go on. I'll find a door that stays a door. I always said I'd leave by one that shut after me."

choices.label: Go to the glass and the preparator, the way down, lighter

blocks.s: He comes down from the gallery without hurry and stands across the bench from you, an old man in a curator's dark coat gone green at the cuff, dry-skinned, dry-eyed, the look of one who has read every soul that came down this stair and been surprised by almost none. He sets a thin catalogue on the bench between you and lays a pen across it.

"I am the Keeper," he says. "I decide what this house keeps. Understand the post and you will understand the night. What the museum finds worthy, it keeps: lit, labelled, looked at, never once let go. The rest it deaccessions, and strikes from the catalogue, and that is the end of them; no entry, no rail, no eye to fall on them ever again, as though they had never come in off the step."

He does not pick up the pen yet. Before any verdict, his hands come together once, a single slow clap that is not applause, the sound a man makes to mark that he has seen a thing and means to weigh it.
"Stand where the light falls. I am going to tell you some things you will wish you had come down too late to hear."

choices.label: Ask him what the museum finds worthy
Ask him who set this exhibition
Refuse to be read; you came for a likeness, not a verdict

blocks.s: You stand at the glass and you tell him the truth, which is that you do not know what you want. That you have seen the cost and it is too heavy to lift, both halves of it, the forgetting you cannot walk into and the keeping you cannot bear to choose, and that you would rather, God help you, someone bigger than you set it, so the choosing is not yours and the weight of it never lands on you.
He goes still. The count stops.

"I told you," he says, low, ashamed already of where his hand is going. "I told you not to come to me unsure. You've come wanting me to be the bigger thing. Wanting it let down so it's the house did it, not you." His hand finds the cleat. "One. I'm built for exactly this. The certainty. Two. And I'll tell myself it was a mercy, that you couldn't have borne the cold street and the long forgetting, and I'll be lying, and we'll both know it. Hold." His eyes are wet and his jaw is set. "Last chance. Take your own self down that stair and choose like a person. Or stand there."

You stand there. You let him. There is a great and terrible relief in it, the relief of a thing you will never have to own. "Three," he says, and his voice breaks on it, and he lets the chain go. The glass comes down over you smooth as poured plaster, fixing you in it, the pose held, the choice made, set from outside, never yours, never to be yours, the one mercy the house was always most glad to grant.

"What it finds worthy." He squares the catalogue a hair to the bench, an exact man. "Here is the part every soul gets backwards. I do not keep the poor likenesses. The poor ones I strike; out they go, unremembered, into the street and the years, to grow old and lose by inches everything the years take, and be let go from every mind that ever held them, which is the common lot and a kind of mercy, though none of them thank me for it. The good ones, the worth-keeping ones, those I keep. Behind glass. In the light. Forever. A thing worth remembering is not a thing you let fade."
The pen taps the catalogue once, a slow clap held to a single beat.

"That is the museum's appetite, and I am its hand. I keep what is worth keeping. I make no apology; an appetite that apologises is only a hunger in good manners." A dry breath. "You are afraid I will find you wanting and turn you out. You have it exactly upside down. To be found wanting is the only way back up that stair and out the door. Pray I am not impressed."

choices.label: Ask who set this exhibition, then
Ask what he is, under the coat

blocks.s: You go into the cold bay and crouch where the child can see you, and you make yourself look, which is the hardest thing you have done in this house, harder than the desk, harder than the double on the plinth, because those were things done to you and this is a thing you let happen.

The child is not grown. It was not let to grow; the remembering closed over the top of it and it stayed exactly here, the age it was the last day before the forgetting started. It has been here the whole length of the kept night, held perfect, while the one who could not let it fade has long since faded themselves, and the world has gone on and let it go and kept no memory of it but this, behind glass, at the back of a cold room no visitor is brought to.

It looks up at you. The dull balloon drifts against your arm and taps. The small mouth moves, twice, the same shape twice, the only question it ever learned to ask in the cold with no one to answer: is it real.

You tell it yes. You do not soften it and you do not perform it; you say, out loud, in your own ordinary voice, that it is real, that the child was real, that the years it never got were real and would have been its own. You take the cold small hand. You do not promise to free it; you cannot, not from here. You only witness it, which is the one thing no one ever did. To be kept has a face now, and a cost you can name in full: this is what never being let go does to a person. You will carry it down to the vault whatever you choose.

choices.label: Climb out of the cold, carrying what you saw

gated.aside: You read the years the likeness never let the child have, the days it was kept perfect instead of let to grow and be lost and be let go, and you understand at last that to be remembered like this is not to be saved from anything. It is only to be stopped.

blocks.s: You ask him what he is, under the curator's coat, under the post. It does not offend him. It brings him the nearest he comes, the whole cold hour, to something almost kind.

"Under the coat." He sets the pen down. "There is no under, with me, and that is the difference between us. You came down this house looking for the likeness of who you stopped being, the person beneath the part the world was letting go. I have not had a person under mine in longer than the catalogue runs. I am the verdict. I am the museum's judgement of what is worth keeping, given a coat and a stand and a steady hand, and a thing like that cannot keep a self; a self has wants, and a judgement with wants is only a grudge with a label."
For a moment the dry eyes hold something that is not dryness.

"I was cut from a larger hunger, one that could not bear to do its own deciding any more and wanted a colder hand on the pen. I am a fragment of that appetite, fitted to a bench. I tell you this not to frighten you but so that when I name your worth you will know it is not I who wants you kept. It is the thing I was struck from. I am only the part of it that learned to be exact."

choices.label: Ask who set this exhibition

blocks.s: You go along the wall where the moulds are racked, and up close they are worse than the figures upstairs, because a figure at least faces out and wears a life. These face the wall. You turn one on its rack, gently, and the inside of it is a face in reverse, every line of a person sunk into white plaster, the hollow where a cheek was, the dent of a closed eye, a mouth pressed in and held. A maker has chalked a number on the back, and a date, and on some of them a small word: KEPT. On a few, scored through hard: STRUCK.

You reach, without deciding to, along the rack for your own face among them, sunk in white, and your hand finds only the blank backs of strangers, and then a gap where a mould has been lifted out and not put back, the dust ghosting a square the wrong size to be comforting. You set your hand down.

There is a working habit you could keep to here, the same as the preparator's: handle them, set each one back square on its rack, mind the order, stay a person with a job in your hands and not a person standing still to be looked at. It is a small thing. It keeps you moving.

choices.label: Keep to the work, handle them and set them straight
Go on to the bench
Go to the catalogue stand
Step back into the dark edge of the room

blocks.s: You keep refusing, and you refuse from the warm soft place and not the awake one; you did not keep to the work, you let the room have your hands and your feet, and so when you stand at the bench and tell the Keeper his pen is none of your business, you tell him in the voice of someone the house has already half got. You make, you think, a fine defiance of it. It is, you understand too late, the most worth-keeping thing you have done all night, and your audience is the one soul in the house that decides what is kept.
He hears all of it, patient, the pen resting in his old hand. When you are done he does not argue.

"A spirited refusal," he says, dry, and not unkindly. "The trouble with refusing the verdict in front of the man who keeps it is that the refusing is the truest reading I get of you, and this one reads beautifully. Defiant. Whole. Worth keeping." The pen moves at last, slow, and fills the blank beside your name that has gone unfilled this hanging and the last and the one before, one word in the KEPT column you do not get to see, and he closes the catalogue on it with a small soft finality. "There. You wished to be no business of mine. You have made yourself my best accession in a hundred years. The museum keeps what it finds worthy, and I find you worthy, and so it keeps you, behind glass, in the light, with the rest of the good ones. You may go up to your case now. You will find the door does not let you through. That is the difference between being struck and being kept; the kept ones reach the door and the door simply holds them, like a held breath, forever, found worthy, remembered, and never let out nor let go."

You put your hand to the push-bar and you go, now, before the bench and the verdict and the cold bay and all of it, out the staff door into the bare stone and the draught, up a service stair, and out a plain door into the wet black street, the cold ending behind you the moment the door swings to.

You did not stay to hear what you were. You did not go down to the vault, did not stand before the blank case, did not learn the whole of the cost or weigh the choice the deep dark was waiting to ask. You only left, fast, while the leaving was easy, which it was, because the way out always works both ways for a soul who has not yet been set.

And because you left unmade, with your likeness half-taken and your verdict unfilled and the cold thing at the back of the workshop unwitnessed, you go out into the street and the years not freed, exactly, and not kept, exactly, but unfinished, a person who came to a museum to find out who they were before they began to be forgotten and turned back at the last cold door without finding out. The forgetting that was coming comes anyway, out here, by inches, the ordinary way, and you meet it the ordinary way, never having looked at the one true thing you came all this way down to look at, never having made the one choice that was yours to make. The door shuts after you. It does not open again. You are out, and you do not, in the end, know what you walked out of.

It does the rare thing then, the thing it has not done since you came into the house. It stops finishing your words and reaches, slow, for one of its own, and you can see what it costs, a strain in a flat voice that has not strained in a hundred exhibitions.

"I am going to say a true word," it says. "Mine. Not a label, not your next phrase, not the same patter I have read off these rails since I forgot which gallery I first walked. A true one. It takes from me to spend it; there is not a great deal of true left in me and I will be the less for it after. So hear it once." Its hand turns on the stand, slow, deliberate, each word paid for. "The one who keeps this house. Who shows you every likeness and pours the welcome and lights the green lamps and cannot ever close the doors. Ask why a thing builds a museum of perfect memory and stands in none of it. Ask what such a one paid, and what it paid in, that it has no likeness any glass will hold, no label, and one case in the rotunda that stood empty before the first accession and will stand empty after the last. The keeper who is in no catalogue. The one case that was never filled. It is struck from every list because it struck itself off, long ago, to buy out of an ending it could not face, and the striking took its remembrance with it. I can say no more. There is no more in me. But you will not be able to think the Keeper is the whole of it, after this."
Its finger comes off the stand. It does not lift its eyes. Somewhere very low there is a sound like a thing that has paid for speaking and is resting.

choices.label: Leave it to its rest and go to the bench

blocks.s: You cannot. You stand at the edge of the cold bay with the lamp throwing its one reach of yellow into it and the small still shape down there on the stool, and the Keeper waiting for you to go in and look, and you find there is a limit to what you came down here able to bear, and you have reached it one cold room short.

"I would rather not," you say, and your voice does the old thing, finds the steady weight and sets it down just so, calm rising in you easy and warm because the calm is the very thing that exists so you never have to feel the rest. "I know it's there. Let that be enough. I don't need to go into the cold and take its hand to know the cost."

The Keeper looks at you a long moment, and something in the dry old face settles, not unkindly, into a verdict he has written many times. "As you wish," he says. "Most cannot. It is not weakness; it is the calm, doing the one thing the house cast it to do." He lifts the lamp away from the bay, and the dark closes over the small still shape, and you are spared the sight of it, which is what you asked for, and what it costs.
