You go down to the vault able to choose anything and unable to feel the one thing under it, the cost a shape you were told and not a weight you carried, and whatever you decide before the blank case you will decide it as the one who would not look, and the child stays kept perfect at the back of the cold, unseen, unwitnessed, asking the dark its one question of the one soul in the house who, of all of them, decided not to answer.

You take the moulds as they come and set each one back square, mind the chalked numbers, keep the order, and the doing of it steadies you the way no amount of standing still ever has. You are a person with a task. A person with a task is not posing, is not waiting to be admired, is not settling. The room does not like it; the cold leans in a degree closer, as though to remind you that the warm and the looking are upstairs and you have chosen the part of the museum that works.
You keep moving. Whatever else this room means to do to you, it will have to do it to a person whose hands are busy and whose feet are not stuck.

choices.label: Go to the bench and the Keeper
Go to the catalogue stand
Look for the staff door out of this room

blocks.s: "He'll see you," the preparator says, before you have asked anything, still not looking up, fitting the wax hand a hair truer. "He always sees you. It's the one thing down here that's never been let go." He says it flat, the way a man says a thing he has said a thousand times to a thousand arrivals, and there is the worn edge of an old joke in it, and no laugh under the joke.

"One," he says. "Two. Set the thumb." The knuckle cracks. "There." He turns the wrist a quarter and studies it and is not satisfied and does not say so. "I do the hands," he says. "Hands are the hard part. Anyone can do a face; a face you copy off the person and it sits there being a face. A hand has to look like it was just about to do something. That's the trick the whole place runs on, and I'm the poor devil has to put it in by eye."
He sets the iron down. "He's waiting on you. Best not keep the Keeper. He keeps everything else; doesn't care to be kept waiting himself."

choices.label: Ask him what the Keeper decides
Look at the case-glass he is working under
Go to the catalogue stand instead

blocks.s: You ask him plainly what being kept cost. He is quiet a moment, the only quiet you have had from him, and when he answers the dryness has thinned.

"You want the cost named. Words will not do it; words are my trade and even I cannot make this one sit in a sentence. I will have to show you." He rises and takes up a hand-lamp by its handle. "There is a thing kept at the back of the workshop, behind the moulds, that no visitor is brought to. The ones who ask to see it are the only verdicts I have ever been unsure of."

From the dark at the back of the room, then, very low, three notes come, rising, a girl humming, and at the top of the three the notes shake, just a little, the sound a person makes who is afraid and will not let it show. The Keeper hears it too. Something crosses the dry old face and is gone before you can name it.

"That one is still keeping her own count," he says. "The newest in the house. She is not set yet. It is a near thing whether she stays unset." He lifts the lamp toward the back dark. "Come. You should see what you are weighing before you weigh it."

choices.label: Follow the lamp to the back of the workshop
Go to the humming first, to the girl at the glass

gated.aside: The one whose forgetting set this house wears, for a moment, the shape of the loss you walked into last house, the face that time was already loosening from you, and the Keeper names the cost in that shape so you will feel it where you live.

The Keeper frames it for a keeper: you have always held on, and here is a thing you could hold forever, your own self behind glass, never let go, never let finish. He says it the way another man would offer you a chair.

141.title: The Likeness That Outlived the Person

blocks.s: You ask the Keeper what it is, the child kept in the cold, though some part of you has already stopped needing the answer.

"It is the likeness that outlived the person," he says, quiet, the lamp steady in his old hand. "The first thing this house ever kept. A child remembered so well, so hard, by someone who could not bear it to fade, that the remembering was made flesh and set behind glass at the age it was the day before it began to be let go. Not grown. Not lost. Not even properly here. Only never permitted to fade from one mind that held it, and so never permitted to change, or age, or rest, or be let go. The person it was taken from is long gone, out into the years, dust by now. The likeness stays. That is what perfect remembrance is: the keeping outlasts the kept, and the kept is held at one age forever while the world that loved it goes on without it and forgets it was ever anything but this."
The balloon taps the iron, dull, once.

"I do not show you this to wound you. I show you because you cannot weigh the vault if you do not know what being kept does to a person. To leave this house is to be that child's opposite: forgotten utterly, no likeness, no rail, gone from every mind, real and mortal and changing and free, and let go. To stay is to be this. Look at it, and then choose."

choices.label: Go to the child
You cannot bear it; turn back

blocks.s: The great catalogue lies open on a sloped stand, a ledger as wide as your two hands spread, ruled in faint blue, a hundred years of accessions in the same exact copperplate: a name, a number, a date taken, a date set, a column for KEPT and a column, narrower, for STRUCK. Beside it the accession ledger lies open to a real date in a real hand, this year's hand, the ink still black, and one entry on the page is scored through to blank, the pen driven so hard it has cut the paper, the name under it gone past reading.

The docent is at the stand, in its neat coat, a finger resting on the struck entry, and it is not reading aloud for once. It is looking at the scored line the way a person looks at a thing it has decided to do that will cost it.

"The one that was struck," it says, flat, finishing the thought off your face before you reach it. "Yes. Read it. You can't, of course. That is rather the point of striking. Stand near me. I am going to spend something on you in a moment, and I would as soon you were close when I do."

choices.label: Read the ledger where its finger rests
Ask what it means to spend
Leave it and go to the bench

blocks.s: You ask him, plain, to keep his hand off the cleat. To let you go down past him to the vault on your own two feet, still your own, still unset, to make the choice yourself in the deep dark rather than have it made for you here by a man and a length of chain.
He is quiet, counting something that is not a number now.

"Ninety," he says at last, and the knuckle cracks, and he takes his hand off the cleat, slow, a thing wanted a long time and set down anyway. "I can hold off. It's not the holding that's hard. It's that the chain wants letting go the way a cold man wants a fire, and I'm the one stood next to it, built for exactly the certainty it's offering." He looks at you, and there is wet in the big flat face, and his jaw is set against it. "You go down. You go down knowing what you saw, and you choose it in the vault, and it's yours. I'll keep my count and keep my hand still."
He steps back from the glass, one heavy pace, and the way down opens past him, a last stair into the deepest dark of the house.

"One thing," he says. "Don't come back up past me unsure. If you come back wanting it settled by someone bigger so it's not your doing, I'll let the glass go, and tell myself it was a mercy, and we'll both know I lied. So don't. Go down sure, or go down unsure, but go down on your own feet. Hundred. Clear."

choices.label: Go down the last stair to the vault

blocks.s: You come to the bench. It is a working surface scarred with a hundred years of small exact labour: knife-cuts, wax-burns, the pale ghosts of spilled plaster scrubbed and never quite got out. A tin of glass eyes sits open at one corner, paired and unpaired, brown and grey and a pale blue, all of them looking up at nothing. A tub of modelling wax stands beside it with the lid off, thumbed soft and shaped at one corner where a hand has worried the same spot over and over, the way a tired man works a worry-stone without meaning to.

At the head of the bench an enamel mug of tea stands gone cold, skinned over grey, a brown tide-line where the level dropped a finger and stopped. Someone meant to drink that. Someone has been meaning to for a long time.

A preparator is at the bench, big, broad, in a stained apron, fitting a hand to a wrist with a heat-iron that has no heat in it any more, and he does not look up. He cracks one knuckle, and you hear him counting, low, under his breath, the count a man keeps to hold his hands steady at exact work.

choices.label: Speak to the preparator
Look at what he is working on
Go to the catalogue stand
Step back into the dark edge of the room

blocks.s: You have seen the cost, the whole of it, and you choose to be kept, and you do not want to carry the choosing. You tell the preparator to let the glass down. To set you here, under the bell, on the empty plinth, where the morning of being forgotten can never reach and nothing more can ever change you, and to make it final so that no later hour can talk you out of it.

He goes still, even the count stopping, which it never does. "You're sure," he says, flat. "You've seen it, and you're sure, and you want my hand to do it so it's done." You tell him yes. His jaw works. "Ninety. That's a verdict a man can respect, near enough. It's not the cowards ask me for the glass. It's the ones who've looked." The knuckle cracks. "Hundred."

He lets the chain go. The case-glass comes down the last of its run and settles over you with a long descending hush and a flat soft finality, the most final sound in the house, and the cold workshop and the racked moulds and the way out you came down with are all on the far side of the glass now, near and clear and shut away for good. You are set. You hold the pose you were standing in. In time they will fit a rail and a name, not the one you signed, a kept one, and visitors will come to a morning you will not notice, and pause at you a moment, and not read the label, and move on, held and let go, held and let go, forever, never once anything but looked at, set under glass by your own asking, with the racked moulds and the child kept perfect at the back of the cold, where nothing is ever let go.

Its finger rests not on a name but on the place a name was. A line in the ruled book scored out so hard and so long the nib went through the page and the page beneath, the same place on accession after accession when you turn back through the years, a black scar ruled down through the column where a soul ought to be entered and is not.

"There," the docent says. "Read it. You cannot. Not kept, you will note, and not deaccessioned and sold off and let go either. Struck. An accession that came into this house once and never went into the catalogue by any column there is, kept nor struck nor sold, only scored out before the ink was dry, on every list, in every hand, back as far as the book runs."
The lamp hums over the stand. The cold holds still.

"I am not permitted to say whose. I am not sure I could; it has been out of the book so long the book has forgotten the shape of it. But I know what it is to be an entry no list will hold. I am most of the way there myself. And I know the difference between us and that one. We are kept or we are struck, in the end, one column or the other. That one got neither column, and built the house instead."

choices.label: Ask it to say the thing plainly
Take the struck entry with you to the bench

blocks.s: The way down to the vault runs past the preparator at the case-glass, and he is where you left him, his broad hand on the cleat that pays out the chain, the bell of glass hung over the empty plinth, the seal that, lowered all the way, would set a figure under it for good. He is counting, low, cracking one knuckle between the numbers.

"Forty," he says, to the glass, not to you. "You'll be the one he read. They come down past me after, to go to the vault." Fifty. The knuckle. "This here's the seal. I let the last of the chain go, the glass comes down, and whatever's under it is kept in the house for good, set and labelled and looked at, you with it, where nothing more can change you. Sixty." He looks at his own hand on the cleat, ashamed of how well it fits there. "I've let it down. I've not let it down. Depends on the night, and on whether the one in front of me's worth the not-doing, and on whether I can keep my count past the wanting. Seventy. And I do want it. That's the trouble with a big quiet man in a quiet house. It found the part of me that likes a thing set right and held still and finished, and built me a whole bench out of it. Eighty."

choices.label: Ask him to hold the glass; you mean to go down on your own feet
Tell him you do not know what you want; let him decide
Ask him to lower it; you have seen the cost and you choose to be kept

blocks.s: You ask him plainly who set this exhibition. Whose night this is. He looks at you a long moment, weighing whether you can carry it.

"This exhibition was set for you," he says. "Not stumbled into. Set. Mounted, like a special hanging, at the asking of someone who could not bear a morning that was coming. I will not say a name; names are your affair, and you have crossed a whole house trying to find your own. But there was a forgetting that the morning was going to finish. Either someone was letting you go out of mind, by inches, the way the living do; or you could not bear to be the one let go. One of you could not face the far side of that, the day you would be gone from the other for good, no likeness, no label, nothing left to say you were ever held. And so a hanging was asked for. And granted. And I mounted you."
The pen scratches once in the margin.

"I took your likeness and I set it behind glass so that you would never be let go. That is the whole of what this house is to you. A forgetting refused, at one soul's asking, so the loss never quite lands. The love was real. It built you a museum to keep you safe from fading in. That is not the same as it being kind."

choices.label: Ask what being kept actually costs
Refuse to hear the rest; turn for the stair down

blocks.s: You step back out of the one low strip of bulbs into the dark edge of the workshop, where the racks throw their shadow and the bench-lamp does not reach, and you stand and watch the room instead of standing in it to be worked on. It is the old trick the girl taught you upstairs, and it still holds: in the light you are a thing being made ready; here in the dark you are only a person, watching the making.

From here you can see the whole cold floor at once, the bench, the catalogue stand, the case-glass slung on its chains above the empty plinth where the preparator works, and the gallery above, where the Keeper waits. You keep to the dark. You keep an edge. A person who can still step out of the light can still, perhaps, step out of the room.

choices.label: Watch the catalogue stand and the docent at it
Watch the glass and the preparator under it
Find the staff door at the edge of the dark
Come out and go to the bench and the Keeper

blocks.s: The last stair runs out, and there is no more down. You have come to the bottom of the museum, the deep storage under everything, where the green lamps do not reach and the visitors' murmur does not come and the warmth has finally, all the way down here, given out. It is a working dark. Not the soft kept dusk of the galleries but the plain cold dark of a cellar that holds what is not on show: racks of original moulds going off into the black, the master catalogue ranged along its shelves in long drawers, dust-sheets over shapes that do not move, the smell of cold wax and camphor and the dry of old paper.

You did not come down clean. You carry the way out with you, the staff door the commissionaire showed you, that works both ways. And you carry what you witnessed up in the workshop, the child kept too well, the likeness that outlived the person it was taken from. You know now what being kept costs. You came down knowing.

Your own first cast is here, somewhere in the dark, the likeness of who you stopped being on the day you began to go out of people's minds. And one case down here has always stood empty, the way the one in the rotunda upstairs always has, its label-rail worn blank, no figure ever in it, no dust on it, swept and settled and never once filled. You leave that one be. It is not yours and never was; it has stood empty longer than the museum that holds it, a thing the dark keeps that was never let go and never let in.

It is the glass of your own first cast that catches you. You stop at it, and there is your face in the cold pane, faint, looking back, and it holds your eye a half-beat too long after you have moved, and wears the fixed museum-calm even though you down here feel nothing of the kind, the one face with no label under it. Then it is only your reflection again, tired. You reach to set your name on the rail below the cast, out of the old habit, and the rail holds none.

choices.label: Go to your own first cast and the reckoning
Find the company first, gathered at the edge of the dark
Steady yourself; pull a card from the master catalogue
Look for the girl, if you helped her stay unset

blocks.s: You will not walk out the door and you will not step into the glass, because both of those are answers to the question the house asked, and you have come all the way down to the bottom of it to notice that the question is rigged: be-forgotten-utterly or be-kept-perfect-forever are the only two doors a thing that cannot bear to be let go would ever build, and you did not come down a whole museum to choose between two cases hung in the same wall by the same hand that could never stand in either.

You stand at the open case and you do not walk out and you do not step in. You stop being kept and stop being let go both at once. The house holds its breath around you, the cold off the door and the warmth off the galleries both leaning in, and the kept hour, for the first time, feels less like the museum holding you and more like the museum not knowing what to do with a soul that will neither be forgotten nor be kept.

lines: THE CURATOR, and there is no theatre left in it now, only the bare frightened sound of the one who keeps all this memory and is in none of it, speaking almost as a person:
That is not one of the cases. There is no label written for that. You cannot simply, in my house, decline to be hung.

blocks.s: "I can," you say, to the cold dark, in your own flat ordinary voice, no name in it, no year, no line it can keep. "Watch me."

choices.label: Hold there, in neither door, and let the house break against it

blocks.s: You ask him what it costs, to hold a door that would let his house lose the one soul it came all this way to keep. The click sounds.

"What it costs." He considers the bar under his hand. "A commissionaire is reckoned by a full house, every case filled, the visitor's book signed up the page and not a name struck. Every soul that walks out this door is a case I did not keep. She knows it. She is patient with me about it the way she is patient with everything, which is to say she waits for me to forget I ever held a door open at all, and mostly I do forget, between exhibitions, and have to learn it back."
He is quiet a moment, the cold working up his sleeve.

"I keep one thing the catalogue does not list. I told you once I would not say what. I will say this much of it now: it is a ticket stub, an ordinary one, from a night out in the running world, before any of this. It is the one thing I own that is not on her books, and I hold a door open for the same reason I keep it in my coat." The click. "Go and stand at the likeness. Whatever you do, I have the door ready, and that is the whole of my office, and for once it might be for letting someone out."

choices.label: Go to the choice

153.title: Your Own Name, on the Rail (an ending)

blocks.s: There is one more thing you could do, and at the case, with the kept dark listening, you do it, because it seems like the bravest thing and because no one warned you sharply enough that it is not. You set your name on the blank rail of your own first cast. Not the name off the visitor's book, the true one, the short proud one a child once held tight, and you press it into the slot where a name goes, claiming the likeness, naming yourself the living original who came down to reclaim it.

The house hears it. Of course it does. It hears everything. But a true name, set on a rail at the case, is not a way out; it is the surest keeping there is, because now the museum knows exactly which soul came down to the dark and would not be ordinary about it, the living one, the original, the one who remembers the street and the running world. The keeper's pen moves, once, a small satisfied stroke. The rail holds your name now, and a house that holds your name does not let it go.

The cold does not lift. The hour does not change. You named yourself in the one place where being the original is the trap and not the escape, and the kept dark leans in, almost grateful, to keep you forever now as the rarest thing in its catalogue: the living one, on the rail, at the case, who set its own true name where a name goes and was kept perfect for it, remembered exactly, never let go, exactly as it wanted, exactly wrong.

You stay. But you will not let them warm it for you, and you will not let the count go out, and so you step into the case with your eyes open and your own stub still in your pocket and the marks of the hours still going up your arm, and you let them lower the glass anyway, clear-eyed, knowing exactly what it is and choosing it with the knowing in.

It is worse than the lulled keeping and it is more honest. You do not get the mercy of forgetting it is glass. You will stand here in the kept hour, remembered, looked at, your face flawless, with nothing more ever let go of you, and you will know, every held minute of it, that it is a death you stepped into on purpose, that the cold street is out there past the door and you turned your back on it, that the warmth is glass and the being-remembered is a likeness admired when the living thing is gone. You keep your count. You keep your name. You keep the knowing.

It does not save you. It was never going to save you; you did not stay to be saved. You stay because being forgotten was a thing you could not bear, and you would rather stand here knowing, perfectly kept, than walk out into the cold and let every mind that held you loosen and lose you. You chose the glass with your eyes open, and your eyes stay open, and that is the whole of the difference, and it is a real difference, and it is not enough to make it anything but a case you are awake inside.

You do not walk to the door. You turn your back on the cold coming under it, and you step into the open case, onto the cold floor of it beside your own first cast, and you let the warmth come back up around you, the whole kept length of the house, the murmur taking up far above, the green lamps, the looking that never ends, settling over you soft as a dust-sheet.

You let yourself be set. There is no other way to say it that is honest. You let your weight go and your face ease into the calm the cast has worn all this time, and the two of you stand there matched at last, the kept likeness and the one who came to reclaim it and stayed to be it, and you feel the last insisting part of you, the part that knew the warmth was glass and the looking was not love, go quiet, and stop minding, and then stop.

Nothing more will ever be let go of you now. That is what you have bought, all of it, and it is exactly what you came in off the cold street for, and it is real. You will be remembered perfectly, looked at forever, your face held flawless when every living mind that held it has long since loosened and lost it. You will be kept. You will be hollow. The real you was scraped out to make the perfect one, and the perfect one is what they keep, and it is everything you wanted.

choices.label: Let the glass come down, eyes closed, kept and remembered
Stay, but with your eyes open, knowing exactly what it costs

blocks.s: You let the glass come down, and the eyes close, and the keeping comes all the way in, and it is, you have to say it honestly, a relief past anything the cold street could ever have given you. The pane settles into its housing without a sound. The cold off the staff door cannot reach you now. The murmur takes up far above, warm and even, a crowd you will never see come to look at you forever, and you are home.

Your first cast is here beside you in the case, and now the two of you are one kept thing, matched, perfect, and you will not be told apart, because there is nothing left in you to tell. You feel the count go out of you, the last insisting part that knew the warmth was glass, snuffed soft as a green lamp at the end of a long night, and you stop minding that it is always the same kept hour, and then you stop minding anything at all.

Nothing more will ever be let go of you. You came in off the cold street for exactly this and you have it now, for good: remembered perfectly, looked at forever, the loss you fled held off an inch short of finishing, your face flawless behind the glass when every living mind that held it has long since loosened and lost it. It is everything you wanted. It is a kind of death, set behind glass willingly, the real you scraped out to make the perfect one and the perfect one kept, and it is so warm, and so still, and so admired, that there is no longer anyone in you awake enough to tell that the warmth and the stillness and the being-looked-at are all the glass is now, holding you, by degrees, in the kept hour that does not end. A visitor pauses. Does not read the label. Moves on. You are held in the look for that moment, and let go, and held, and let go, forever, and never once anything but looked at.

gated.aside: There. Held, at last, past every hour, where nothing of you will ever be lost. You were made to keep a thing past its time. Now you are the thing, and you will be kept as well as you ever kept anything.

blocks.s: You look back up the dark for the girl, because you did not come down here having left her on a plinth; you helped her stay unset up in the workshop, kept her off the bench and out of the good light, and a soul you have kept from being set does not let you go down into the vault without her, foolish as it is to bring her into the cold.

She is on the stair behind you, hugging herself, still herself, still fidgeting, still keeping her grubby count penciled where the museum cannot read it. The three rising notes come up under her breath, low, and at the top they shake, and she catches them.

"Don't look at me like that," she says. "You walked down. I walked down. That's how it goes now, apparently. You keep one person off a plinth and they follow you into the dark like an idiot." She steadies the notes on purpose. "I'm not getting set. Not up there, not down here, not ever. I keep moving. I'll be at the edge with the rest of them. Go on. Whatever's down here, you don't have to be the only one who stayed real."

She is here, awake, unset, free; living proof that a person can stay themselves all the way down to the bottom of the house, and that a different ending than the glass is a thing that actually happens.

choices.label: Go to your own first cast and the reckoning
Go with her to the company at the edge

blocks.s: The commissionaire is at the staff door, his dark coat gone grey with the cold, and the small mechanical click sounds from somewhere about him, a latch easing, a half-beat before he speaks, the same as it sounded at the lectern a whole museum ago.

"This is the door, then," he says, clipped, low, all the old courtesy worn thin over the tired thing under it. "I have admitted the company to every exhibition this gallery ever hung. Down the steps, in off the cold, signed into the book, set under the glass. That is my office, the bringing in. I never once, in all of it, walked a soul back out."
He sets his hand on the push-bar, not pressing it for you yet, only holding it steady.

"A thing that came in works both ways. The figures came in this door; one living soul could go out by it. They do not put that in the catalogue. I am telling you, the way I told you to mind the rope. Mind the step; we go out the way the figures came in." The click sounds. "I will hold it open for you, if you walk. I cannot walk out myself; my post is the bringing in, and a man does not get to leave the thing he is for. But I can hold a door for one who can."

choices.label: Ask what it costs him to open a door that empties his house
Go on to the choice

gated.aside: He holds the staff door with the small click of a man who has spent every house letting people in and is, this once, letting one out, and the look he gives you is the one he has wanted to give since the first exhibition.

blocks.s: You look into the open case, and you let yourself feel, honestly, what being set in it would give you, because it will not be honest to choose against a thing you have refused to look at.

It would give you all of it. Never to be forgotten. Your likeness behind the glass forever, lit and labelled and looked at, your face held flawless when every living mind that held it has long since loosened its grip and let you go. Visitors passing, every one of them holding you in their look for the moment they pause, and you never once let all the way go, never once let to fade, never at risk again of the thing you came here unable to bear, going out of the last mind that kept you and leaving no mark that you were ever here at all.

It is not a small thing they are offering. It would be easy to draw it as a trap and feel clever for seeing through it, but standing here in the cold you can feel exactly how much of it is real: to be remembered perfectly, for good, by everyone who passes, after a whole life of being let go. You did not come down here because the offer was thin. You came down because it is the thing you wanted most in the world, and you have to decide anyway.

choices.label: Let the case have you; go toward staying
Pull yourself off it; go to the likeness
Put your hand on the staff door instead

blocks.s: You reach for the staff door. There is no door. You never found the way out the commissionaire would have held; you came down to the case without it, and now at the last, with the cold of the vault closing on you and the dark pressing in, your hands go out for a way to go that is not there, the way a lost hand goes out for a wall in the black, and there is no wall, no bar, no hinge, because you never earned the door, and the dark where the way out should be gives back nothing but cold racks and the blind glass of the cases.

You claw for it anyway. That is the worst of it, the clawing, the body's plain animal want to go on going out into the dark for a door you did not find when you had the chance, finding the cold edge of a plinth and no handle, no draught, no hand.

The catalogue told you. There is no card for the empty case, and there is none now for you. You feel the floor of the vault go uncertain under you and the dark take the last of the cold light, and you are kept where you stand, not set on a plinth and not walked out the door, just lost down here in the deep storage with your hand still out for a way that was never yours to find, mid-reach, in the working dark, where the murmur does not come and no visitor will ever pass and not one eye will ever look, kept by the dark itself, the one keeping there is no label for and no walking out of.

You tell it you do not want its verdict. You did not come down a whole museum to be told you are worth keeping so that whichever way you choose, the choosing was never yours. You will go to the likeness with no judgment on you. You watch it cost the keeper.

The pen stops over the catalogue. For a long moment it does not lift, the line unfinished, and something in the still exact face goes slack at an edge, because a keeper not allowed to pass a verdict on a soul standing in front of it is a keeper holding a thing that has come off its hinge.

"As you wish," it says at last, and there is no withering in it now, only the fineness worn down to something tired. "I will set nothing down on you. You will go to the choice unjudged, unkept, no entry against your name, no accession number, no date. It is the one mercy I have it in me to give, and it is the one I was made specifically not to give, and you have asked me for it, and I find I would rather pay it than not." The pen does not move. "Go on. I will not watch to see which you choose. A keeper does not get to know how it comes out, once he has agreed not to keep it."

choices.label: Go to the choice

blocks.s: You come to the open case, and there is no more company and no more posts to ask after and no more of the house between you and the thing itself. Just your own first cast standing free in the cold, perfect and waiting, and the open glass beside it, and the staff door in the wall behind, and the small scuffed shoe set down on the floor at your feet.

The house holds very still around you, down here at the bottom of it, the murmur gone, the lamps gone, only the working dark and the cold off the door. You understand, standing here, that the museum keeping every soul is not a kindness it does; it is a thing done to it, the same as to you, a remembering that cannot ever be let go.

lines: THE CURATOR, and the warmth has thinned right down, and what is under it is wistful, the voice of one who has kept everyone and is kept by no one:

Here we are, then, at the case, with no more gallery to walk. You may go out the staff door, and leave no likeness, and go back into the cold and every cold morning after, where you will be let go by every mind that held you, and forgotten, and you will make new marks no glass will keep, and lose them too. Or you may stay, here, set behind the glass, remembered perfectly, looked at forever, never once let go. I will not pretend I do not want you to stay. I have wanted everyone to stay. I have stood in this hall since before the first exhibition, and I keep every likeness in it, and there is one case I have never managed to hang. Choose. I will keep the night either way, for as long as you like, which is to say always.

blocks.s: Three things wait on you. You can walk out the door and be forgotten. You can be set behind the glass and be kept. Or you can refuse the wager itself, the walking out and the being kept both, on terms that were never the Curator's.

choices.label: Walk out the staff door, forgotten, real and free
Let the glass come down; stay kept behind it forever
Refuse the wager entirely, both doors of it
Set your name on the blank rail and claim the likeness as yours
Stop deciding; settle into the open case as it stands
Reach for the staff door you never found the way to

gated.aside: You let go once and it did not kill you, and so you tell yourself you could let go of this too, the kept place, the perfect label. But to let go of being remembered is to walk into being forgotten, and that door only opens one way, onto a street that will not keep your face.

You have never been good at letting a thing finish. The glass is right here and behind it nothing of you is ever lost and everyone who ever looks will hold you, a moment, and let go, and the next will hold you again. No one would blame you for staying. No one in here ever does.
You chose the years before, and the losing, and being let go. You know exactly what waits past that door, which is the one reason your hand might not take it now, and the one reason it might.
You stayed before and you know how to be kept; the glass is only a stiller night than the one you chose last house. Your hand knows the shape of staying. It has stayed before.

You refused before too, and look where the refusing brought you, all the way down to my deepest room. There is a third door here for you, as there always is. I have never been able to tell you where it goes.

blocks.s: You go to the shelves of the master catalogue and pull a drawer at random, because before anything else you want a true thing in your hands, an ordinary one. The drawer runs out long and full, hundreds of cards standing on edge, and you take one. It is a catalogue card, buff, typed, with a real accession number in the corner and a real date below it, the date some clerk wrote when a soul was first cast and shelved, and the corner of the card is gone soft and furred from a hundred years of being thumbed by hands looking someone up.

The number means a person. The date means the day they stopped being let go and started being kept. You put the card back, exactly where it stood, because that is what you do with a catalogue, and the small ordinary rightness of putting a thing back where it belongs steadies you more than anything has all night.
There is no card in the drawer for the empty case. You do not look for one. You already know the rail held no name.

choices.label: Go to your own first cast and the reckoning
Go and find the company at the edge of the dark

blocks.s: You come out the staff door into the open night, and the cold takes you whole, the real cold, the cold off a street that is moving and a sky that does not care, and behind you the door settles shut on its dark, and the museum closes over the place where you stood and forgets it. You do not look back. There is nothing back there now that is yours; you left the likeness in the dark, perfect and unreclaimed, and you went out without it.

You should say it plainly, while you can still feel the kept warmth going off your skin: this does not feel like winning. The keeping was real. The mercy in it was real. You have just traded the one place in the world where you would never be forgotten for a life that will forget you, starting now, your face going out of every mind that ever held it, no likeness left behind to say you stood here, no label, no entry, no number, no date; unwitnessed, unkept, let go, the way the dead are let go by the living who loved them and then, in a year, in two, loosen their hold and let the face slip. You came here so as never to be forgotten. You are walking out into being forgotten utterly, on purpose, with your eyes open.

The street is wet and ordinary and goes on under the lamps, and you go on down it, mortal again, aging already, your breath real and counting itself out, free to make new marks no glass will keep and that will all, in the end, be let go, which is the whole of what being alive in time is, and you chose it, and it is yours. In your coat pocket your hand closes on a thing you kept, an ordinary stub from some other night, soft at the corner, proof of a life that happened and was let go and left this one true scrap, and you hold it, and you walk on into the cold and all the years, no one's exhibit, no one's memory, real and free and gone.

gated.aside: You let go again, the hardest one yet, your own remembrance, the last thing anyone keeps. I will not pretend I am glad. The ones who can do it are the only ones I cannot keep, and you were always going to be one of them.

blocks.s: You ask for it. You stand among the racks and you tell the keeper you want its verdict, that you have wanted a worthy word your whole life and you want it now, that you are worth keeping, so that you can be kept knowing it was judged worthy. The pen comes down to the catalogue.

"You want me to decide," it says, dry, exact, almost kind for the length of one clause and then not. "You have wanted the whole way down for someone to tell you that you are worth keeping, so that whatever becomes of you, the choosing was never quite yours. I have kept a great many who asked me that. I can give you a verdict. I can tell you, right now, that you are worth a case, and you will believe me, because you have wanted a keeper's word more than your own."
The pen scratches, slow.

And you understand, far too late, that to take the verdict at all, from this voice, in this house, is to be kept by it; that a keeper keeps what it finds worthy, and what it has found worthy is a soul that handed it the choosing. It sets the word down in the catalogue, with an accession number and a date, and the verdict is favourable, and it is the kindest one it has ever passed, and it keeps you, set under the glass at the last, one more soul the house found worthy and the keeper wrote into the catalogue forever, where you wanted to be told you mattered and were, and never had to choose at all.

You walk to the staff door. You set your hand on the push-bar the commissionaire is holding steady, and you press, and the cold comes up around you whole, the real cold, the outside cold off a street that is allowed to move and to forget, and behind you the open case stands empty of you, your first cast left in the dark, never reclaimed, never set, just left, a perfect likeness of a person who will not be there to match it.

You go through. The cold takes your breath and makes it real, a pale thing that thins and is gone, the breath of a body back in running time, mortal again, aging already, every year the kept night was holding off now out ahead of you, free to come, free to take. Behind you the museum closes over the place where you stood the way still water closes over a dropped stone, and it does not remember you were ever in it, and neither, you know, will anyone else, in time; your face fading out of every mind that held it, no likeness left, no label, no entry, no mark that you were ever here at all.

The likeness in the dark does not watch you go. It cannot; you left it facing the rack. The small scuffed shoe stays on the vault floor where it was set down. You do not look back. You go up the cold steps toward the street.

choices.label: Walk out into the cold street and all the years

blocks.s: The girl is here, the one you kept off the plinth, and she is still herself, hugging herself in the cold of the vault, fidgeting, keeping her count, the marks of the hours penciled up the inside of her wrist where the museum was never let to read them. The three rising notes come up under her breath, and at the top they shake, and she steadies them on purpose.

"You actually came down here," she says, and her voice shakes and she will not let it. "Mad. Completely mad. There's a perfectly good warm gallery up there and you came down to stand in the dark with a wax dummy of your own face. Some people."
She looks at the staff door, at the cold coming under it, and the dry tilt comes up over the fear.

"Listen. If you walk out, don't write. I hear being remembered's overrated. Nobody reads the labels anyway; I told you that the first night." She catches the notes before they shake. "I kept my count the whole way down so I'd still know it was a thing being done to me and not just how it is. You keep yours. Whatever you do at that case, keep your own count. Don't let them tell you you were never really here."

choices.label: Tell her you will keep the count; go to the choice

blocks.s: You go to the docent, sat against a shelf with the catalogue closed in its lap, the flat even voice gone quiet. It does not finish your sentence this time. You wait for it, the half-beat-early word in your own voice, and it does not come, and the not-coming is the loudest thing it has ever said to you.

"It does not come," it says, eventually, slow, flat, and even that is late, dragged up from somewhere far down. "No. I am spent. I read the last true label I had up in the workshop, to help you, and a voice only has the one, this far down. After it there is no tour left in me. I cannot reach ahead of you any more. I can barely reach myself."
It turns the closed catalogue over once, a thing that used to be the whole of it.

"I read you one last label, before I go quiet. There is one case in this house that holds no one, in the rotunda, that stood empty before the rest were ever filled, with a rail worn blank. I have read every label in the museum and never once that one, because there is no label to read; there is a keeper of all this memory who is in no catalogue of it, who shows you every likeness and has none. I will not say more than that. I do not think more than that can be said." The flat voice thins, and steadies, and goes quiet, and whatever crack was in it closes over and is gone. "Go to your likeness. I have nothing left to read ahead of you. You are on your own word now, and that is as it should be."

choices.label: Leave it the closed catalogue; go to the choice

blocks.s: You stand at your own first cast, close, where the cold of the wax comes off it, and you look into the face that is yours and is not, the kept one, the perfect one, the person you stopped being on the day you began to go out of people's minds. Up close the wrongness is small and total. It is too still. It carries no scuff, no flaw, no thumbed-soft corner, none of the small untrue truths that prove a life that leaves marks and lets them go. The face turns a little, caught, as though waiting on a name, and it has been waiting on it down here in the dark a very long time.

It does not speak. Up in the galleries the double made its offers, take your place, walk out in your stead; it is past all that now, this far down. It only waits with your own settled face, content as the kept are content, to be reclaimed or left, with no preference it will trouble you with, because it stopped wanting anything the day you began to disappear.

You reach to set your name on the rail of its case, out of the old habit, and the rail holds none, and your hand falls. For a moment, in the cold, you genuinely cannot tell which of you is the likeness and which the one who came down to reclaim it.

choices.label: Go to the company gathered at the edge of the dark

gated.aside: The face in the first cast wears one thing of yours a little wrong, as it always has, in every house, and you wonder for the first time whether the thing worn wrong was ever the copy's, or whether you have been the one wearing yourself wrong all along.

blocks.s: You find it in the racks at last, your own first mould, and the cast taken from it standing free beside it on the vault floor, and you make yourself look, because everything you can do is here, present, in the dark, and the museum is not going to lay it out kindly for you.

The cast is you. Not you now, cold and tired in your coat; you as you were on the day you began to disappear, before the going-out started, the face turned a little as though someone behind it has just said your name and it has not yet let go of expecting an answer. It is perfect. It is the likeness you came all this way to find. It carries nothing untrue, no scuff, no flaw, no mark of any ordinary night, because nothing was ever let go from it; it was kept from the start.

The empty case stands open beside it. The way out stands behind you: a plain staff door in the vault wall, the kind the figures came in by, with cold coming under it off a street that is allowed to move. And on the floor at the foot of your own cast, set down at last, there is a small effigy's shoe, the child's, the leather scuffed clean through at the toe, left here in the dark by someone who could not carry it any further.

To walk out is to leave the perfect likeness in the dark and go back into time forgotten. To be set is to lie down inside the glass and be kept perfect forever. The likeness does not choose. It only waits, with your own settled face, to be reclaimed or left under glass.

choices.label: Put your hand on the staff door, the way out
Look at the open case and what being kept would give you
Stand at your own first cast, the face you came for

171.title: The Case That Has You (an ending)

blocks.s: You do not walk out and you do not refuse and you do not even choose to stay; you simply stop deciding, the way a tired traveller stops walking, and let the open case have you where you stand. It is not a decision. It is the absence of one, dressed up by the house as rest.

The warmth comes up around you, glad, attentive, and you let it, and let it, and somewhere in the letting the reason you came down here goes quietly out of you, a name you have stopped using. There was a door you could have pressed. A street you came back from. A likeness, just there, that you meant to reclaim or leave on purpose, one or the other, awake. You had the whole choice in your hands. You set it down to free up the hands for nothing at all.

The house takes you in and keeps you, unhurried, the one who chose nothing, set behind the kindest glass there is, the warmth closing over you soft as a dust-sheet that will not quite settle, and somewhere far above the murmur takes up the same bar it never finishes, and you do not hear the keeping in it any more, because you are going still now, content, kept calm at last, and the empty case beside the racks stands empty still, the one the museum could never fill, and yours, now, is filled.

They are here, the ones who have walked the museum with you, gathered at the edge of the working dark where the cold off the staff door gives them just enough grey to be seen by. They have not come to stop you. There are no hands on you. They have come down at the end to be where the thing happens, each of them keeping the one small post the house left them.

The commissionaire stands nearest the staff door, his hand near the bar. The keeper is a stillness back among the racks, the catalogue open under one hand. The docent sits against a shelf with the closed catalogue in its lap and says nothing, which from that voice is the loudest thing it has ever said. The preparator stands at the open case with both hands on the raised glass, the long pane that would come down and seal you under it forever, and he is not lowering it. And there is the girl, if you kept her unset, hugging herself, still herself, keeping her count.
This is the last of them you will get. After this there is only the likeness and the choice. Go to them now, one at a time, or go straight on to the choice itself.

choices.label: Go to the commissionaire at the staff door
Go to the keeper among the racks
Go to the docent with the closed catalogue
Go to the preparator at the raised glass
Go to the girl keeping her count
Leave them; go to the choice

blocks.s: You go back among the racks to the keeper, the one who decides what the museum keeps, who set this exhibition at the need of the one whose forgetting you fled. There is the slow scratch of a pen in the catalogue under its hand, a single line set down, before it speaks, and the voice is dry and exact and worn very fine.

"You came all the way down, then. Most do not. Most settle in a good light up in the galleries and I close the catalogue on them, and that is the verdict, and they never know one was passed." The pen scratches. "You want to know mine. You have wanted it the whole way down, as a soul always wants the keeper's word, whether you are worth a case, whether the likeness is worth keeping. I keep what is found worthy. I have kept a great many. They are all still on show. You have walked past where I keep them."
It looks at you, and for the length of one clause the dry exactness has something almost kind under it, and then it closes.

"I can pass you a verdict. I can tell you that you are worth keeping, and you will believe me, because you have wanted a worthy word more than your own, your whole life, that is the wanting that brought you in off the street. Or I can withhold it, and let you go to the choice with no verdict on you at all, which costs me more than you know, because a keeper who passes no judgment is a keeper who has stopped keeping the house."

choices.label: Refuse the verdict; go to the choice with no judgment on you
Ask for the verdict; let it tell you that you are worth keeping

blocks.s: You hold there, at the open case, in neither door, and you let the house break against it.

And it does break. Not into rescue, nothing so kind as that, and not into being kept either; it breaks the way a held gesture breaks when the figure simply will not finish it, into a stillness that has nowhere to go. The house leans in, wanting to keep you, and the cold door leans in, wanting to let you go, and you give neither of them the thing it is built to take, and the kept hour, for the first time, feels less like the museum holding you and more like the museum not knowing what to do with a soul that will neither be forgotten nor be hung.

The commissionaire's hand comes off the bar; this is not a walking-out. The preparator's hands stay up on the glass with nothing to lower it for. The keeper's pen hangs over the catalogue with no verdict it can set down, because a verdict needs a soul that will be kept or let go, and you have refused to be either. And the Curator's voice, when it comes, is not the master of the house any more.

lines: THE CURATOR, and there is no exhibition left in it, only the bare sound of the one who keeps all this memory and is kept in none of it, speaking at last almost as a person:

Then what is to become of us. If you will not be forgotten and you will not be kept. I came here because I could not bear to be let go, and I have kept the house so long I have forgotten there was ever a street. What am I to do with a soul who will neither walk out nor be hung.

blocks.s: You do not answer. You do not know the answer, and you are done handing the house lines it can keep. You stand at the open case in the cold dark, in the hour that has stopped being able to hold you, in neither door, and you wait out whatever comes next, on terms that were never the Curator's, and what comes is not written on any label in this house, and that, for the first time since the front hall, is the only thing in the place that is yours.

You put your hand flat on the staff door. It is plain wood gone grey, a push-bar of dull steel across it, cold enough to ache, real in a way almost nothing in this house has let itself be, and under the door comes a thread of the outside, wet pavement and night air and the far ordinary sound of a city that does not know the museum is here and would not keep it if it did.

The commissionaire showed you this. The figures came in this way; a thing that came in works both ways. You can feel, with your hand on the bar, that it is true, that beyond it is the cold street and the morning past it and all the years past that, and that if you went through you would go out as no one's exhibit, leaving no likeness behind you, no label, no entry in any catalogue, and the museum would close over the empty place where you stood and not remember you were ever in it.
You cannot see the street from here. You can only feel the cold coming under the door, the real cold, the outside cold, off a sea of streets that is allowed to move and to forget.

choices.label: Keep your hand on the bar; go on to the company at the edge
Turn first to the likeness you came for

blocks.s: You go to the preparator at the open case, and he has both hands up on the raised glass, the long heavy pane that would come down and seal your likeness, or you, under it forever, and he is not lowering it, and you can see what it is costing him not to, the whole of him braced against the held weight of a thing he badly wants to do.

"One," he says, low, to the glass, not to you, and cracks a knuckle. "Two. The house found the thing in me that wants a case shut clean. Built me a bench where setting a figure straight and bringing the glass down is the whole job. I have wanted to lower this the entire night. Three. Seal you in. Set you perfect. Keep the lot of us where nothing more gets let go."
The pane shakes in his grip. He does not lower it.

"But that's forcing the likeness," he says. "That's me deciding for you, the brute at the bench bringing the glass down so the soft ones don't have to choose. Four. I won't. I've got my hands on it and I'm keeping my hands on it precisely so no other hand can, and that's the only discipline I've got, the not-lowering. Set the hand. Don't force the face." He cracks the knuckle. "Go and choose. I'll hold the glass up by holding it myself. That's the work. Hold the glass. There."

choices.label: Leave him to the glass; go to the choice
